kids.question: Who tried to make himself king while David was old?
kids.option_a: Adonijah
kids.explanation: 1 Kings 1:5 — "Then Adonijah the son of Haggith exalted himself, saying, I will be king."

kids.question: Who did King David choose to be the next king of Israel?
kids.option_a: Solomon
kids.explanation: 1 Kings 1:30 — "Even so will I certainly do this day." David confirmed Solomon as king.

kids.question: Where was Solomon taken to be anointed as king?
kids.option_a: Gihon
kids.explanation: 1 Kings 1:33 — "The king said...cause Solomon my son to ride upon mine own mule, and bring him down to Gihon."

teens.question: Who were the two people who went to King David to report Adonijah's attempt to take the throne?
teens.option_a: Nathan and Bathsheba
teens.option_b: Joab and Abiathar
teens.option_c: Zadok and Benaiah
teens.option_d: Shimei and Abishai
teens.explanation: 1 Kings 1:11 — "Wherefore Nathan spake unto Bathsheba the mother of Solomon," and they together approached King David.

teens.question: Which priest anointed Solomon as king at Gihon?
teens.option_a: Zadok
teens.option_b: Abiathar
teens.option_c: Eli
teens.option_d: Ahimelech
teens.explanation: 1 Kings 1:39 — "And Zadok the priest took an horn of oil out of the tabernacle, and anointed Solomon."

teens.question: What did Adonijah do when he heard that Solomon had been anointed king?
teens.option_a: He caught hold on the horns of the altar
teens.option_b: He fled the country
teens.option_c: He gathered an army to fight
teens.option_d: He went into hiding
teens.explanation: 1 Kings 1:50 — "And Adonijah feared because of Solomon, and arose, and went, and caught hold on the horns of the altar."

teens.question: Which notable supporters did Adonijah NOT have on his side?
teens.option_a: Zadok the priest and Benaiah and Nathan
teens.option_b: Joab and Abiathar
teens.option_c: The men of Judah
teens.option_d: The king's servants
teens.explanation: 1 Kings 1:26 — "But me, even me thy servant, and Zadok the priest, and Benaiah...and thy servant Solomon, hath he not called."

teens.question: What was the name of the young woman brought to minister to the aged King David?
teens.option_a: Abishag
teens.option_b: Bathsheba
teens.option_c: Rizpah
teens.option_d: Tamar
teens.explanation: 1 Kings 1:3 — "And they found Abishag a Shunammite, and brought her to the king."

adults.question: What condition prompted David's servants to seek a young woman to minister to the king?
adults.option_a: He could get no heat though they covered him with clothes
adults.option_b: He was bedridden with a fever
adults.option_c: He had lost his sight
adults.option_d: He was unable to eat
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 1:1 — "Now king David was old and stricken in years; and they covered him with clothes, but he gat no heat."

adults.question: Adonijah's attempt to seize the throne echoes which earlier sibling's pattern?
adults.option_a: Absalom, who also prepared chariots and horsemen
adults.option_b: Amnon, who was firstborn
adults.option_c: Shephatiah, the third son
adults.option_d: Chileab, who died young
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 1:5-6 — "He prepared him chariots and horsemen...And his mother bare him after Absalom," mirroring Absalom's earlier rebellion.

adults.question: What specific oath had David sworn to Bathsheba that Nathan referenced to spur his intervention?
adults.option_a: That Solomon would reign after him and sit upon his throne
adults.option_b: That her son would be high priest
adults.option_c: That Solomon would build the temple
adults.option_d: That Bathsheba would be queen mother
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 1:13 — "Didst not thou, my lord, O king, swear unto thine handmaid, saying, Assuredly Solomon thy son shall reign after me, and he shall sit upon my throne?"

adults.question: What instruments and shout accompanied Solomon's anointing procession?
adults.option_a: Pipes, and the people rejoiced with great joy so the earth rent
adults.option_b: Trumpets and cymbals and harps
adults.option_c: Tabrets and psalteries and singing
adults.option_d: Horns and drums and dancing
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 1:40 — "And all the people came up after him, and the people piped with pipes, and rejoiced with great joy, so that the earth rent with the sound of them."

adults.question: On what condition did Solomon allow Adonijah to live after he held the altar?
adults.option_a: If he showed himself a worthy man, no hair of him would fall
adults.option_b: If he left Jerusalem immediately
adults.option_c: If he swore an oath before Zadok
adults.option_d: If he renounced all claim to the throne publicly
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 1:52 — "If he will shew himself a worthy man, there shall not an hair of him fall to the earth: but if wickedness shall be found in him, he shall die."

adults.question: What did Joab's support of Adonijah reveal about the political alignment at David's court?
adults.option_a: A faction of the old military guard backed Adonijah while the prophetic and priestly circle backed Solomon
adults.option_b: All the court unanimously supported Adonijah
adults.option_c: The Levites alone opposed Adonijah
adults.option_d: Joab acted alone without allies
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 1:7 — "And he conferred with Joab the son of Zeruiah, and with Abiathar the priest: and they following Adonijah helped him."

adults.question: What was the name of the feast Adonijah held where he proclaimed himself king?
adults.option_a: A sacrificial feast at En-rogel with sheep, oxen, and fat cattle
adults.option_b: A Passover feast at Gibeon
adults.option_c: A peace offering at Bethel
adults.option_d: A new moon feast at Hebron
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 1:9 — "And Adonijah slew sheep and oxen and fat cattle by the stone of Zoheleth, which is by En-rogel."

adults.question: What was David's blessing upon Solomon when he charged him from his bed?
adults.option_a: Blessed be the LORD God of Israel, which hath given one to sit on my throne this day, mine eyes even seeing it
adults.option_b: The LORD make thee wise above all men
adults.option_c: Keep the commandments and thou shalt prosper
adults.option_d: Go in peace and rule with justice
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 1:48 — "And also thus said the king, Blessed be the LORD God of Israel, which hath given one to sit on my throne this day, mine eyes even seeing it."

kids.question: What did King David tell Solomon to do before he died?
kids.option_a: Keep the commandments of the LORD
kids.explanation: 1 Kings 2:3 — "And keep the charge of the LORD thy God, to walk in his ways, to keep his statutes, and his commandments."

kids.question: What happened to Adonijah when he asked for Abishag to be his wife?
kids.option_a: Solomon had him put to death
kids.explanation: 1 Kings 2:25 — "And king Solomon sent by the hand of Benaiah...and he fell upon him that he died."

kids.question: Where did Joab flee when he knew Solomon was after him?
kids.option_a: Into the tabernacle to hold the horns of the altar
kids.explanation: 1 Kings 2:28 — "Then tidings came to Joab: for Joab had turned after Adonijah...and Joab fled unto the tabernacle of the LORD, and caught hold on the horns of the altar."

teens.question: What promise did David remind Solomon to fulfil regarding the sons of Barzillai the Gileadite?
teens.option_a: Let them eat at Solomon's table because they showed kindness to David
teens.option_b: Give them land in Judah as a reward
teens.option_c: Make one of them a commander in the army
teens.option_d: Pay them the wages David had owed them
teens.explanation: 1 Kings 2:7 — "But shew kindness unto the sons of Barzillai the Gileadite, and let them be of those that eat at thy table."

teens.question: For what act did David charge Solomon to deal wisely with Joab?
teens.option_a: Joab had shed the blood of war in peace, killing Abner and Amasa
teens.option_b: Joab had stolen from the royal treasury
teens.option_c: Joab had conspired with foreign kings
teens.option_d: Joab had burned the harvest fields
teens.explanation: 1 Kings 2:5 — "Moreover thou knowest also what Joab the son of Zeruiah did to me...how he shed the blood of war in peace, and put the blood of war upon his girdle."

teens.question: Why did Solomon consider Adonijah's request for Abishag treasonous?
teens.option_a: Asking for the king's woman was equivalent to asking for the kingdom
teens.option_b: Abishag was Solomon's betrothed
teens.option_c: Abishag was a royal priestess
teens.option_d: It violated a covenant David had made
teens.explanation: 1 Kings 2:22 — "And why dost thou ask Abishag the Shunammite for Adonijah? ask for him the kingdom also; for he is mine elder brother."

teens.question: How many years did Shimei live peacefully in Jerusalem before he violated Solomon's oath?
teens.option_a: Three years
teens.option_b: Two years
teens.option_c: Five years
teens.option_d: One year
teens.explanation: 1 Kings 2:39 — "And it came to pass at the end of three years, that two of the servants of Shimei ran away."

teens.question: What condition had Solomon set for Shimei's life to be spared?
teens.option_a: He must not cross the brook Kidron
teens.option_b: He must dwell in Jerusalem and not go elsewhere on pain of death
teens.option_c: He must serve in the temple
teens.option_d: He must pay a yearly tribute
teens.explanation: 1 Kings 2:36-37 — "Build thee an house in Jerusalem, and dwell there...For it shall be, that on the day thou goest out...thou shalt know for certain that thou shalt surely die."

adults.question: What theological framework did David give Solomon for his reign in his final charge?
adults.option_a: Walk in God's ways, keep His statutes and commandments as written in the law of Moses, so the LORD may continue His word
adults.option_b: Build the temple and offer sacrifices daily
adults.option_c: Conquer all surrounding nations to secure the kingdom
adults.option_d: Maintain alliances with Hiram and Egypt
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 2:3 — "Keep the charge of the LORD thy God, to walk in his ways...as it is written in the law of Moses, that thou mayest prosper in all that thou doest."

adults.question: What specific dynastic promise did David remind Solomon of concerning God's covenant?
adults.option_a: There shall not fail thee a man on the throne of Israel if thy children walk before God in truth with all their heart
adults.option_b: The kingdom shall stand forever unconditionally
adults.option_c: Ten tribes would always remain loyal
adults.option_d: The temple would never be destroyed
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 2:4 — "That the LORD may continue his word which he spake concerning me, saying, If thy children take heed to their way, to walk before me in truth with all their heart."

adults.question: What was Abiathar's punishment for supporting Adonijah, and why was he not executed?
adults.option_a: He was thrust out of the priesthood but spared because he had borne the ark before David
adults.option_b: He was imprisoned for life
adults.option_c: He was exiled to a foreign land
adults.option_d: He was demoted to a minor temple role
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 2:26-27 — "And unto Abiathar the priest said the king, Get thee to Anathoth...because thou barest the ark of the Lord GOD before David."

adults.question: How did Benaiah execute Joab inside the tabernacle, and what scripture rationale did Solomon give?
adults.option_a: He killed him there, removing innocent blood from Israel and David's house that Joab had shed
adults.option_b: He dragged him outside first at Solomon's command
adults.option_c: He waited for a priest's permission
adults.option_d: He offered Joab a last chance to surrender
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 2:31-33 — "Go fall upon him...that thou mayest take away the innocent blood...from David, and from his seed, and from his house, and from his throne, for ever."

adults.question: How did Solomon's removal of Abiathar fulfil an earlier prophecy?
adults.option_a: It fulfilled God's word spoken concerning Eli's house at Shiloh
adults.option_b: It fulfilled Nathan's oracle given during David's reign
adults.option_c: It fulfilled Moses' warning about corrupt priests
adults.option_d: It fulfilled Samuel's last prophecy
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 2:27 — "So Solomon thrust out Abiathar from being priest unto the LORD; that he might fulfil the word of the LORD, which he spake concerning the house of Eli in Shiloh."

adults.question: What did Shimei do that violated his oath and led to his death?
adults.option_a: He went to Gath to bring back two runaway servants
adults.option_b: He spoke against Solomon publicly
adults.option_c: He attempted to leave Israel
adults.option_d: He harboured enemies of the king
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 2:40 — "And Shimei arose, and saddled his ass, and went to Gath to Achish to seek his servants: and Shimei went, and brought his servants from Gath."

adults.question: How does the summary verse close the account of Solomon's consolidation of power?
adults.option_a: So the kingdom was established in the hand of Solomon
adults.option_b: And there was peace throughout all Israel
adults.option_c: Solomon reigned in wisdom and justice from that day
adults.option_d: The LORD blessed Solomon above all kings
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 2:46 — "So the king commanded Benaiah the son of Jehoiada; which went out, and fell upon him, that he died. And the kingdom was established in the hand of Solomon."

adults.question: What legal principle did Solomon invoke from Moses' law when ordering Shimei's death?
adults.option_a: The guilty person who commits evil shall bear their own iniquity, and the curse returns on their own head
adults.option_b: Two witnesses are required before any execution
adults.option_c: No man shall be put to death for his father's sins
adults.option_d: The king's word is law above Mosaic statute
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 2:44-45 — "The LORD shall return thy wickedness upon thine own head...And king Solomon shall be blessed, and the throne of David shall be established before the LORD for ever."

kids.question: What did Solomon ask God to give him?
kids.option_a: An understanding heart to judge the people
kids.explanation: 1 Kings 3:9 — "Give therefore thy servant an understanding heart to judge thy people, that I may discern between good and bad."

kids.question: Two women both claimed a baby was theirs. What did Solomon say he would do to find the truth?
kids.option_a: Divide the living child in two with a sword
kids.option_b: Ask the women to take an oath
kids.option_c: Pray for God to reveal the true mother
kids.option_d: Watch which woman the baby reached for
kids.explanation: 1 Kings 3:25 — "And the king said, Divide the living child in two, and give half to the one, and half to the other."

kids.question: Who was the real mother revealed to be?
kids.option_a: The woman who said do not kill the child
kids.option_b: The woman who agreed to divide the child
kids.explanation: 1 Kings 3:27 — "Then the king answered and said, Give her the living child, and in no wise slay it: she is the mother thereof."

teens.question: Where did God appear to Solomon in a dream at the start of chapter 3?
teens.option_a: Gibeon
teens.option_b: Jerusalem
teens.option_c: Bethel
teens.option_d: Shiloh
teens.explanation: 1 Kings 3:5 — "In Gibeon the LORD appeared to Solomon in a dream by night: and God said, Ask what I shall give thee."

teens.question: What did Solomon acknowledge about himself when God invited him to ask for anything?
teens.option_a: He was a little child and knew not how to go out or come in
teens.option_b: He lacked military strength
teens.option_c: He had not yet built the temple
teens.option_d: He was younger than all the kings around him
teens.explanation: 1 Kings 3:7 — "And now, O LORD my God, thou hast made thy servant king instead of David my father: and I am but a little child: I know not how to go out or come in."

teens.question: Because Solomon asked for wisdom rather than riches or long life, what else did God promise to give him?
teens.option_a: Riches and honour, so there shall not be any among kings like him all his days
teens.option_b: Victory over all his enemies
teens.option_c: A son to rule after him
teens.option_d: The love of all the nations
teens.explanation: 1 Kings 3:13 — "And I have also given thee that which thou hast not asked, both riches, and honour: so that there shall not be any among the kings like unto thee all thy days."

teens.question: What did Solomon offer when he returned to Jerusalem after God's appearance at Gibeon?
teens.option_a: Burnt offerings and peace offerings before the ark, and a feast for all his servants
teens.option_b: A prayer of thanksgiving at the high place
teens.option_c: A proclamation of fasting throughout Israel
teens.option_d: Silver and gold to the priests
teens.explanation: 1 Kings 3:15 — "And Solomon awoke; and, behold, it was a dream...and he offered up burnt offerings, and offered peace offerings, and made a feast to all his servants."

teens.question: What was the key difference in how the two harlot mothers reacted to Solomon's sword verdict?
teens.option_a: The true mother pleaded to spare the child while the other agreed to divide him
teens.option_b: One wept and one laughed
teens.option_c: One fled the court and one stayed
teens.option_d: One called witnesses and one was silent
teens.explanation: 1 Kings 3:26 — "Then spake the woman whose the living child was...for her bowels yearned upon her son, and she said, O my lord, give her the living child, and in no wise slay it: but the other said, Let it be neither mine nor thine, but divide it."

adults.question: What political alliance did Solomon make at the opening of chapter 3, and what was its theological note?
adults.option_a: He made affinity with Pharaoh king of Egypt, though the people still sacrificed at high places because no house for the LORD was built yet
adults.option_b: He made a treaty with Hiram of Tyre
adults.option_c: He allied with the king of Moab
adults.option_d: He married a daughter of the king of Syria
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 3:1-2 — "And Solomon made affinity with Pharaoh king of Egypt...Only the people sacrificed in high places, because there was no house built unto the name of the LORD."

adults.question: What specific description of David does Solomon use to ground his own unworthiness before God?
adults.option_a: David walked before God in truth and righteousness and uprightness of heart
adults.option_b: David was a man after God's own heart
adults.option_c: David kept all the commandments perfectly
adults.option_d: David had built the altar at Gibeon
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 3:6 — "Thou hast shewed unto thy servant David my father great mercy, according as he walked before thee in truth, and in righteousness, and in uprightness of heart with thee."

adults.question: What was the nature of Solomon's request — what exact phrase did he use for the gift he sought?
adults.option_a: A hearing heart to judge God's people, to discern between good and bad
adults.option_b: Wisdom surpassing all other kings
adults.option_c: Knowledge of God's hidden mysteries
adults.option_d: Discernment of every legal dispute
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 3:9 — "Give therefore thy servant an understanding heart to judge thy people, that I may discern between good and bad: for who is able to judge this thy so great a people?"

adults.question: What was the condition God attached to His promise of long life for Solomon?
adults.option_a: If Solomon would walk in God's ways, keep His statutes and commandments as David had done
adults.option_b: If Solomon would build the temple within ten years
adults.option_c: If Solomon would not intermarry with foreign nations
adults.option_d: If Solomon would not raise taxes on the people
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 3:14 — "And if thou wilt walk in my ways, to keep my statutes and my commandments, as thy father David did walk, then I will lengthen thy days."

adults.question: What was the situation of the two harlots' babies at the heart of the dispute Solomon had to judge?
adults.option_a: One baby had died in the night when his mother overlaid him, and each woman claimed the living child
adults.option_b: One baby had been stolen from the market
adults.option_c: One baby was sick and both claimed to be the true mother
adults.option_d: One baby had been swapped at birth in the palace
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 3:19-20 — "And this woman's child died in the night; because she overlaid it...And she arose at midnight...and took my son from beside me."

adults.question: What was the reaction of all Israel to Solomon's judgment in this case?
adults.option_a: They feared the king, for they saw that the wisdom of God was in him to do judgment
adults.option_b: They praised God in the temple
adults.option_c: They marvelled but were uncertain of the verdict
adults.option_d: They compared him favourably to David
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 3:28 — "And all Israel heard of the judgment which the king had judged; and they feared the king: for they saw that the wisdom of God was in him, to do judgment."

adults.question: What does God's offer "Ask what I shall give thee" reveal about the nature of this divine encounter?
adults.option_a: It was an open invitation testing Solomon's character and priorities before his reign was confirmed
adults.option_b: It was a routine blessing given to every new king
adults.option_c: It was a test that all kings of Israel had to pass
adults.option_d: It was a limited offer only available at Gibeon
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 3:5 — "In Gibeon the LORD appeared to Solomon in a dream by night: and God said, Ask what I shall give thee," revealing God's desire to know Solomon's heart.

adults.question: What does Solomon's description of Israel as "a great people, that cannot be numbered nor counted for multitude" recall?
adults.option_a: The Abrahamic promise of descendants as numerous as stars and sand
adults.option_b: The census taken by David
adults.option_c: The population count of the Exodus generation
adults.option_d: The prophecy of Balaam
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 3:8 — "And thy servant is in the midst of thy people which thou hast chosen, a great people, that cannot be numbered nor counted for multitude," echoing Genesis 15:5 and 22:17.

kids.question: How many proverbs did Solomon speak?
kids.option_a: Three thousand
kids.explanation: 1 Kings 4:32 — "And he spake three thousand proverbs: and his songs were a thousand and five."

kids.question: How many songs did Solomon compose?
kids.option_a: A thousand and five
kids.explanation: 1 Kings 4:32 — "His songs were a thousand and five."

kids.question: What did Solomon know about that showed his great wisdom about nature?
kids.option_a: Trees, beasts, fowl, creeping things, and fishes
kids.option_d: The secrets of the deep sea
kids.explanation: 1 Kings 4:33 — "And he spake of trees...he spake also of beasts, and of fowl, and of creeping things, and of fishes."

teens.question: How many officers did Solomon have who provided for the king and his household each month?
teens.option_a: Twelve officers over all Israel
teens.option_b: Seven officers
teens.option_c: Forty officers
teens.option_d: Twenty-four officers
teens.explanation: 1 Kings 4:7 — "And Solomon had twelve officers over all Israel, which provided victuals for the king and his household: each man his month in a year made provision."

teens.question: How many men did Solomon have who were in charge of his chariots and horsemen?
teens.option_a: Forty thousand stalls of horses for his chariots, and twelve thousand horsemen
teens.option_b: Twenty thousand chariots and ten thousand horsemen
teens.option_c: Thirty thousand horses and eight thousand riders
teens.option_d: Fifty thousand horses in all Israel
teens.explanation: 1 Kings 4:26 — "And Solomon had forty thousand stalls of horses for his chariots, and twelve thousand horsemen."

teens.question: Whose wisdom was Solomon's wisdom said to excel?
teens.option_a: All the children of the east, and all the wisdom of Egypt
teens.option_b: The wisdom of the Babylonians
teens.option_c: The wisdom of the Greeks
teens.option_d: The wisdom of the Persians
teens.explanation: 1 Kings 4:30 — "And Solomon's wisdom excelled the wisdom of all the children of the east country, and all the wisdom of Egypt."

teens.question: What description is given about the daily provision for Solomon's household?
teens.option_a: Thirty measures of fine flour and threescore measures of meal, ten fat oxen, and fourscore oxen
teens.option_b: One hundred sheep daily and an equal number of cattle
teens.option_c: Twelve oxen and a hundred sheep for each officer
teens.option_d: A daily feast of seven courses for the whole court
teens.explanation: 1 Kings 4:22-23 — "And Solomon's provision for one day was thirty measures of fine flour, and threescore measures of meal, ten fat oxen, and twenty oxen out of the pastures, and an hundred sheep."

teens.question: How far did Solomon's dominion extend according to chapter 4?
teens.option_a: From the river unto the land of the Philistines, and unto the border of Egypt
teens.option_b: From the Euphrates to the Nile
teens.option_c: From Dan to Beersheba
teens.option_d: From Hebron to Damascus
teens.explanation: 1 Kings 4:21 — "And Solomon reigned over all kingdoms from the river unto the land of the Philistines, and unto the border of Egypt."

adults.question: Who was Solomon's chief priest according to the list of officers in chapter 4?
adults.option_a: Azariah the son of Zadok
adults.option_b: Abiathar
adults.option_c: Zadok himself
adults.option_d: Elihoreph
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 4:2 — "And these were the princes which he had; Azariah the son of Zadok the priest."

adults.question: Who were the scribes or secretaries in Solomon's administration?
adults.option_a: Elihoreph and Ahiah, the sons of Shisha
adults.option_b: Azariah son of Nathan
adults.option_c: Jehoshaphat the son of Ahilud
adults.option_d: Adoniram son of Abda
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 4:3 — "Elihoreph and Ahiah, the sons of Shisha, scribes."

adults.question: What phrase describes the security and prosperity of Israel during Solomon's reign?
adults.option_a: Judah and Israel dwelt safely, every man under his vine and under his fig tree
adults.option_b: There was no war in all the land for forty years
adults.option_c: The people rejoiced in abundance and praised the king
adults.option_d: Israel was the mightiest nation under heaven
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 4:25 — "And Judah and Israel dwelt safely, every man under his vine and under his fig tree, from Dan even to Beersheba, all the days of Solomon."

adults.question: What was the measure of fodder required daily for Solomon's horses and other riding animals?
adults.option_a: Barley and straw for the horses and dromedaries brought to each officer's place
adults.option_b: Ten thousand bushels of grain for each stable
adults.option_c: A daily delivery of twenty cartloads of hay
adults.option_d: Threescore carts of fodder from the royal granaries
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 4:28 — "Barley also and straw for the horses and dromedaries brought they unto the place where the officers were, every man according to his charge."

adults.question: Which four wise men were listed by name as being surpassed by Solomon's wisdom?
adults.option_a: Ethan the Ezrahite, and Heman, and Chalcol, and Darda, the sons of Mahol
adults.option_b: Ahithophel, Hushai, Joab, and Abner
adults.option_c: Hiram of Tyre, the Queen of Sheba, the wise men of Egypt, and Balaam
adults.option_d: Isaiah, Jeremiah, Ezekiel, and Daniel
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 4:31 — "For he was wiser than all men; than Ethan the Ezrahite, and Heman, and Chalcol, and Darda, the sons of Mahol."

adults.question: What does the phrase "God gave Solomon wisdom and understanding exceeding much" imply about the source of his knowledge?
adults.option_a: His wisdom was a divine gift, not a natural or acquired human achievement
adults.option_b: He studied wisdom from Egyptian scrolls
adults.option_c: He inherited David's wisdom through lineage
adults.option_d: He gained wisdom through military campaigns
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 4:29 — "And God gave Solomon wisdom and understanding exceeding much, and largeness of heart, even as the sand that is on the sea shore."

adults.question: How many nations sent tribute and served Solomon throughout his reign?
adults.option_a: All kingdoms from the Euphrates to Egypt brought presents and served Solomon all his days
adults.option_b: Seven vassal kingdoms paid tribute annually
adults.option_c: Twelve nations sent gold and silver
adults.option_d: Twenty-four governors collected tribute
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 4:21 — "And Solomon reigned over all kingdoms from the river...and they brought presents, and served Solomon all the days of his life."

adults.question: What does "largeness of heart" mean in the context of 1 Kings 4:29?
adults.option_a: An expansive capacity of mind and understanding, like the sand of the sea in breadth
adults.option_b: Generosity toward the poor
adults.option_c: A compassionate ruling style
adults.option_d: Physical stamina for royal duties
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 4:29 — "And God gave Solomon wisdom and understanding exceeding much, and largeness of heart, even as the sand that is on the sea shore."

kids.question: Who was the king that sent cedar trees and workers to help Solomon build the temple?
kids.option_a: Hiram king of Tyre
kids.explanation: 1 Kings 5:1 — "And Hiram king of Tyre sent his servants unto Solomon; for he had heard that they had anointed him king in the room of his father."

kids.question: What two kinds of wood did Hiram send to Solomon?
kids.option_a: Cedar trees and fir trees
kids.explanation: 1 Kings 5:10 — "So Hiram gave Solomon cedar trees and fir trees according to all his desire."

kids.question: What did Solomon give Hiram in return for the wood?
kids.option_a: Food — wheat and oil every year
kids.explanation: 1 Kings 5:11 — "And Solomon gave Hiram twenty thousand measures of wheat for food to his household, and twenty measures of pure oil."

teens.question: Why was David unable to build the temple during his own lifetime?
teens.option_a: Because of the wars David had waged, the blood upon his hands
teens.option_b: Because he lacked sufficient materials
teens.option_c: Because God had not yet revealed the temple plans
teens.option_d: Because the people of Israel were not yet settled
teens.explanation: 1 Kings 5:3 — "Thou knowest how that David my father could not build an house unto the name of the LORD his God for the wars which were about him on every side."

teens.question: How many men did Solomon raise as a levy out of all Israel to work in Lebanon?
teens.option_a: Thirty thousand men
teens.option_b: Ten thousand men
teens.option_c: Fifty thousand men
teens.option_d: Twenty thousand men
teens.explanation: 1 Kings 5:13 — "And king Solomon raised a levy out of all Israel; and the levy was thirty thousand men."

teens.question: How were the thirty thousand levy workers scheduled in Lebanon?
teens.option_a: Ten thousand a month in shifts — one month in Lebanon and two months at home
teens.option_b: All thirty thousand went at once for three months
teens.option_c: They rotated weekly in groups of one thousand
teens.option_d: They each served six months and returned
teens.explanation: 1 Kings 5:14 — "And he sent them to Lebanon, ten thousand a month by courses: a month they were in Lebanon, and two months at home."

teens.question: How many burden bearers and hewers in the mountains did Solomon conscript for the temple work?
teens.option_a: Seventy thousand bearers of burdens and eighty thousand hewers in the mountains
teens.option_b: Fifty thousand bearers and sixty thousand hewers
teens.option_c: Forty thousand of each kind
teens.option_d: A hundred thousand workers in total equally divided
teens.explanation: 1 Kings 5:15 — "And Solomon had threescore and ten thousand that bare burdens, and fourscore thousand hewers in the mountains."

teens.question: What special instruction did Solomon give about how the stones for the temple should be prepared?
teens.option_a: They were to be prepared at the quarry so no tool of iron was heard in the house during building
teens.option_b: They should be carved with cedar inlay at the site
teens.option_c: Each stone should be marked with the name of a tribe
teens.option_d: The Levites alone should cut and prepare the stones
teens.explanation: 1 Kings 5:17-18 and 6:7 — "And the king commanded, and they brought great stones, costly stones...And the house, when it was in building, was built of stone made ready before it was brought thither."

adults.question: What was Hiram's reaction when he heard Solomon's message about building the temple?
adults.option_a: He rejoiced greatly and blessed the LORD for giving David a wise son over a great people
adults.option_b: He asked for military alliance before sending materials
adults.option_c: He consulted his own priests before agreeing
adults.option_d: He demanded half of Jerusalem as payment
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 5:7 — "And it came to pass, when Hiram heard the words of Solomon, that he rejoiced greatly, and said, Blessed be the LORD this day, which hath given unto David a wise son over this great people."

adults.question: What condition of peace enabled Solomon to proceed with the temple that David had been denied?
adults.option_a: The LORD had given Solomon rest on every side, with no adversary nor evil occurrent
adults.option_b: Solomon had defeated all surrounding nations in battle
adults.option_c: A treaty with Egypt had secured Israel's borders
adults.option_d: The Philistines had been removed from the land
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 5:4 — "But now the LORD my God hath given me rest on every side, so that there is neither adversary nor evil occurrent."

adults.question: What specific trade agreement did Solomon and Hiram formalise?
adults.option_a: Hiram would supply cedar and fir timber; Solomon would supply wheat and oil annually, and there was peace between them
adults.option_b: Hiram would supply timber and craftsmen; Solomon would pay in gold annually
adults.option_c: A joint military alliance with shared garrisons
adults.option_d: A marriage alliance sealed with Solomon's sister
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 5:11-12 — "And Solomon gave Hiram twenty thousand measures of wheat...and twenty measures of pure oil...And there was peace between Hiram and Solomon; and they two made a league together."

adults.question: How did the timber reach Solomon from Lebanon?
adults.option_a: Hiram made the logs into floats and brought them by sea to Joppa
adults.option_b: Ox-drawn carts carried them overland through Syria
adults.option_c: Levi-appointed porters carried them on their shoulders
adults.option_d: Ships from Ezion-geber sailed them around to Joppa
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 5:9 — "My servants shall bring them down from Lebanon unto the sea: and I will convey them by sea in floats unto the place that thou shalt appoint me."

adults.question: How many overseers did Solomon appoint over the construction workers?
adults.option_a: Three thousand and three hundred who ruled over the people that wrought in the work
adults.option_b: One thousand overseers for each tribe
adults.option_c: Seven hundred officers and three hundred deputies
adults.option_d: Twelve chief overseers, one per tribe
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 5:16 — "Beside the chief of Solomon's officers which were over the work, three thousand and three hundred, which ruled over the people that wrought in the work."

adults.question: What does the term "levy" (Hebrew: mas) indicate about Solomon's labour policy?
adults.option_a: It was a form of compulsory state corvée labour imposed on the Israelite population
adults.option_b: It was a voluntary enlistment programme with pay
adults.option_c: It referred to foreign slaves acquired in conquest
adults.option_d: It was a religious duty fulfilled by each tribe in rotation
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 5:13 — "And king Solomon raised a levy out of all Israel; and the levy was thirty thousand men," indicating forced state labour.

adults.question: What prior relationship had existed between Hiram and David that laid the foundation for this alliance?
adults.option_a: Hiram had always been a lover of David
adults.option_b: Hiram and David had fought together against Philistia
adults.option_c: Hiram had supplied David with materials for the palace in Jerusalem
adults.option_d: Hiram had sent messengers to David after Absalom's rebellion
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 5:1 — "And Hiram king of Tyre sent his servants unto Solomon; for he had heard that they had anointed him king...for Hiram was ever a lover of David."

adults.question: What theological significance does Solomon attach to the temple-building project in his message to Hiram?
adults.option_a: Building a house for the name of the LORD God fulfils what the LORD promised David — that his son on the throne would build it
adults.option_b: The temple would replace the tabernacle as a permanent sign of the covenant
adults.option_c: The temple would be a witness to all nations of Israel's God
adults.option_d: The temple's completion would ensure Israel's military dominance
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 5:5 — "And, behold, I purpose to build an house unto the name of the LORD my God, as the LORD spake unto David my father, saying, Thy son, whom I will set upon thy throne...he shall build an house unto my name."

kids.question: How many years did it take Solomon to build the temple?
kids.option_a: Seven years
kids.explanation: 1 Kings 6:38 — "And in the eleventh year...was the house finished throughout all the parts thereof, and according to all the fashion of it. So was he seven years in building it."

kids.question: What was the most holy room inside the temple called?
kids.option_a: The oracle, or Most Holy Place
kids.explanation: 1 Kings 6:16 — "And he built twenty cubits on the sides of the house, both the floor and the walls with boards of cedar: he even built them for it within, even for the oracle, even for the most holy place."

kids.question: What were the walls of the inside of the temple covered with?
kids.option_a: Cedar wood
kids.explanation: 1 Kings 6:15 — "And he built the walls of the house within with boards of cedar, both the floor of the house, and the walls of the ceiling."

teens.question: What were the dimensions of the temple that Solomon built?
teens.option_a: Sixty cubits long, twenty cubits wide, and thirty cubits high
teens.option_b: Forty cubits long, twenty cubits wide, and twenty cubits high
teens.option_c: A hundred cubits long, fifty cubits wide, and forty cubits high
teens.option_d: Thirty cubits long, ten cubits wide, and fifteen cubits high
teens.explanation: 1 Kings 6:2 — "And the house which king Solomon built for the LORD, the length thereof was threescore cubits, and the breadth thereof twenty cubits, and the height thereof thirty cubits."

teens.question: What remarkable fact is noted about the noise level during the temple's construction?
teens.option_a: There was neither hammer nor axe nor any tool of iron heard in the house while it was being built
teens.option_b: The workers sang psalms all day long
teens.option_c: The people prayed in silence throughout the building
teens.option_d: No animals were used in the construction
teens.explanation: 1 Kings 6:7 — "And the house, when it was in building, was built of stone made ready before it was brought thither: so that there was neither hammer nor axe nor any tool of iron heard in the house, while it was in building."

teens.question: What did God promise Solomon in the middle of the temple's construction?
teens.option_a: If Solomon walked in God's statutes, God would dwell among Israel and not forsake His people
teens.option_b: That the temple would stand for a thousand years
teens.option_c: That no enemy would ever enter Jerusalem
teens.option_d: That Solomon would reign for eighty years
teens.explanation: 1 Kings 6:12-13 — "If thou wilt walk in my statutes, and execute my judgments...then will I perform my word with thee...And I will dwell among the children of Israel, and will not forsake my people Israel."

teens.question: What were the two large carved figures placed inside the oracle (Most Holy Place)?
teens.option_a: Two cherubims of olive tree, each ten cubits high
teens.option_b: Two golden angels with outstretched wings
teens.option_c: Two carved lions of cedar wood
teens.option_d: Two bronze oxen facing the ark
teens.explanation: 1 Kings 6:23 — "And within the oracle he made two cherubims of olive tree, each ten cubits high."

teens.question: What material covered the floor of the temple's main hall and inner oracle?
teens.option_a: Boards of fir, and the floor was overlaid with gold
teens.option_b: Marble tiles from Lebanon
teens.option_c: Cedar planks throughout
teens.option_d: Polished stone from Bashan
teens.explanation: 1 Kings 6:15,30 — "And he built the walls of the house within with boards of cedar...And the floor of the house he overlaid with gold."

adults.question: In what year and month of Solomon's reign did he begin building the temple?
adults.option_a: The fourth year, in the month of Zif, the second month
adults.option_b: The third year, in the first month
adults.option_c: The seventh year, in the month of Bul
adults.option_d: The tenth year after his accession
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 6:1 — "And it came to pass in the four hundred and eightieth year...in the fourth year of Solomon's reign over Israel, in the month Zif, which is the second month, that he began to build the house of the LORD."

adults.question: What was the porch (ulam) before the temple's main hall, and what were its dimensions?
adults.option_a: Twenty cubits long across the breadth of the house, and ten cubits deep in front of the house
adults.option_b: Ten cubits wide and five cubits deep
adults.option_c: Thirty cubits by fifteen cubits
adults.option_d: Forty cubits long and twenty cubits wide
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 6:3 — "And the porch before the temple of the house, twenty cubits was the length thereof, according to the breadth of the house; and ten cubits was the breadth thereof before the house."

adults.question: What were the winding stairs in the temple used to access?
adults.option_a: The middle chamber and the third storey side chambers built against the walls of the house
adults.option_b: The roof of the oracle
adults.option_c: The storage vaults beneath the main hall
adults.option_d: The inner courts for the priests
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 6:8 — "The door for the middle chamber was in the right side of the house: and they went up with winding stairs into the middle chamber, and out of the middle into the third."

adults.question: How wide were the wings of each cherub, and how did they relate to the oracle's width?
adults.option_a: Each cherub had ten-cubit wingspan; together they spanned the full twenty cubits of the oracle
adults.option_b: Each cherub had five-cubit wingspan covering half the oracle
adults.option_c: Their wings were twelve cubits wide and overlapped
adults.option_d: Their wingspan matched the height of the chamber
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 6:24,27 — "And five cubits was the one wing of the cherub, and five cubits the other wing...and the wings of the cherubims were twenty cubits."

adults.question: What carved decorations adorned the cedar walls throughout the main hall of the temple?
adults.option_a: Knops, open flowers, cherubims, and palm trees carved in cedar
adults.option_b: Only the menorah and table of shewbread images
adults.option_c: Scenes from Israel's history in relief
adults.option_d: Geometric patterns and tribal emblems
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 6:29 — "And he carved all the walls of the house round about with carved figures of cherubims and palm trees and open flowers, within and without."

adults.question: What were the doors of the oracle and the main hall made of, and what was carved on them?
adults.option_a: The oracle doors of olive tree with cherubims, palm trees, and flowers overlaid with gold; the main hall doors of fir tree
adults.option_b: Both sets of doors were of cedar overlaid with pure gold
adults.option_c: The oracle doors were of acacia wood; the hall doors of cedar
adults.option_d: All doors were of bronze, with silver overlays for the carvings
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 6:31-34 — "And for the entering of the oracle he made doors of olive tree...And for the entering of the temple he made doors of fir tree."

adults.question: What does the phrase "in the four hundred and eightieth year after the children of Israel were come out of the land of Egypt" situate chronologically?
adults.option_a: It places the temple's foundation in a pivotal redemptive timeline connecting Exodus to the Solomonic era
adults.option_b: It corrects an error in the book of Judges
adults.option_c: It proves Solomon's ancestry from the Exodus generation
adults.option_d: It marks the boundary between the period of the judges and the monarchy
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 6:1 — "And it came to pass in the four hundred and eightieth year after the children of Israel were come out of the land of Egypt...that he began to build the house of the LORD."

adults.question: In what year and month was the temple completed according to the closing verse of chapter 6?
adults.option_a: The eleventh year in the month of Bul, the eighth month
adults.option_b: The tenth year in the month of Zif
adults.option_c: The fourteenth year in the first month
adults.option_d: The seventh year in the month of Nisan
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 6:38 — "And in the eleventh year, in the month Bul, which is the eighth month, was the house finished."

kids.question: What were the names of the two large bronze pillars Solomon placed at the temple porch?
kids.option_a: Jachin and Boaz
kids.explanation: 1 Kings 7:21 — "And he set up the pillars in the porch of the temple...and he called the name thereof Jachin: and he called the name of the left pillar Boaz."

kids.question: How many years did Solomon spend building his own palace?
kids.option_a: Thirteen years
kids.explanation: 1 Kings 7:1 — "But Solomon was building his own house thirteen years, and he finished all his house."

kids.question: What was the name of the large round bronze basin made for the temple?
kids.option_a: A molten sea
kids.explanation: 1 Kings 7:23 — "And he made a molten sea, ten cubits from the one brim to the other."

teens.question: Who was Hiram the craftsman, and who sent him to Solomon?
teens.option_a: A widow's son of the tribe of Naphtali, a worker in brass sent by Hiram king of Tyre
teens.option_b: A Levite appointed by Zadok
teens.option_c: An Egyptian craftsman hired by Solomon
teens.option_d: A Phoenician architect from Byblos
teens.explanation: 1 Kings 7:13-14 — "And king Solomon sent and fetched Hiram out of Tyre. He was a widow's son of the tribe of Naphtali, and his father was a man of Tyre, a worker in brass."

teens.question: How tall were the two bronze pillars, and what topped them?
teens.option_a: Eighteen cubits high each, with chapiters of molten brass five cubits tall
teens.option_b: Twenty cubits high with carved cedar tops
teens.option_c: Thirty cubits high with golden capitals
teens.option_d: Twelve cubits high with lily-shaped crowns
teens.explanation: 1 Kings 7:15-16 — "For he cast two pillars of brass, of eighteen cubits high apiece...and he made two chapiters of molten brass, to set upon the tops of the pillars: the height of the one chapiter was five cubits."

teens.question: How many oxen did the molten sea rest upon, and how were they arranged?
teens.option_a: Twelve oxen — three facing each cardinal direction
teens.option_b: Four oxen, one at each corner
teens.option_c: Eight oxen in two rows
teens.option_d: Six oxen arranged in a circle
teens.explanation: 1 Kings 7:25 — "It stood upon twelve oxen, three looking toward the north, and three looking toward the west, and three looking toward the south, and three looking toward the east."

teens.question: How many lavers did Solomon place in the temple court, and what were they used for?
teens.option_a: Ten lavers to wash in, used for washing burnt offerings
teens.option_b: Twelve lavers for purification rites
teens.option_c: Seven lavers symbolizing completeness
teens.option_d: Four lavers at the corners of the court
teens.explanation: 1 Kings 7:38-39 — "Then made he ten lavers of brass: one laver contained forty baths...and he put five on the right side of the house, and five on the left side."

teens.question: What building in Solomon's palace complex was named after a notable tree used in its construction?
teens.option_a: The house of the forest of Lebanon
teens.option_b: The cedar hall of justice
teens.option_c: The olive-wood throne room
teens.option_d: The fir-tree audience chamber
teens.explanation: 1 Kings 7:2 — "He built also the house of the forest of Lebanon; the length thereof was an hundred cubits, and the breadth thereof fifty cubits."

adults.question: What was the Hall of Pillars and what were its dimensions?
adults.option_a: A porch of pillars fifty cubits long and thirty cubits broad with a porch of judgment before it
adults.option_b: A colonnade forty cubits by twenty cubits for public audiences
adults.option_c: A seventy-cubit hall used for storing weapons
adults.option_d: A ninety-cubit gallery connecting the palace to the temple
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 7:6 — "And he made a porch of pillars; the length thereof was fifty cubits, and the breadth thereof thirty cubits: and the porch was before them."

adults.question: What were the bases (mekonoth) of the lavers made of, and what decorated them?
adults.option_a: Brass bases with lions, oxen, cherubims, wreaths of work, and wheels of brass
adults.option_b: Cedar frames inlaid with gold lions
adults.option_c: Stone pedestals carved with floral designs
adults.option_d: Iron stands overlaid with bronze plates
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 7:29 — "And on the borders...were lions, oxen, and cherubims: and upon the bases so...a work of base work."

adults.question: What was the capacity of the molten sea in terms of baths?
adults.option_a: It contained two thousand baths
adults.option_b: It held five hundred baths
adults.option_c: Its capacity was three thousand baths
adults.option_d: It measured one thousand baths
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 7:26 — "And it was an hand breadth thick, and the brim thereof was wrought like the brim of a cup, with flowers of lilies: it contained two thousand baths."

adults.question: What was the primary purpose of the molten sea according to chapter 7?
adults.option_a: For the priests to wash in
adults.option_b: For purifying the burnt offerings
adults.option_c: For storing water in case of siege
adults.option_d: For ceremonial immersion of the altar
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 7:39 — "And he put five bases on the right side of the house, and five on the left side...and the sea was set on the right side of the house eastward over against the south." (2 Chronicles 4:6 clarifies the sea was for the priests to wash in.)

adults.question: What items in the temple were made of pure gold rather than brass?
adults.option_a: The altar, table of shewbread, candlesticks, flowers, lamps, tongs, bowls, spoons, censers, hinges — all of pure gold
adults.option_b: Only the ark overlay and the cherubim
adults.option_c: Only the candlesticks and the altar of incense
adults.option_d: The pillars Jachin and Boaz alone
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 7:48-50 — "And Solomon made all the vessels...the altar of gold, and the table of gold, whereupon the shewbread was...And the candlesticks of pure gold...and all the vessels of the house of the LORD."

adults.question: What summary statement closes the account of all the temple furniture Hiram made for Solomon?
adults.option_a: Hiram made an end of doing all the work he made for king Solomon for the house of the LORD
adults.option_b: The work was declared very good and blessed by the priests
adults.option_c: Solomon accepted the work and presented Hiram with gifts
adults.option_d: The people assembled to see the completed furnishings
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 7:40 — "And Hiram made the lavers, and the shovels, and the basons. So Hiram made an end of doing all the work that he made king Solomon for the house of the LORD."

adults.question: In what plain did Solomon have all the brass items cast, and why was that location chosen?
adults.option_a: In the plain of Jordan between Succoth and Zarthan, in the clay ground
adults.option_b: In the valley of Hinnom outside Jerusalem
adults.option_c: Near the copper mines of the Arabah
adults.option_d: In the wilderness of Sinai near the Tabernacle's former site
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 7:46 — "In the plain of Jordan did the king cast them, in the clay ground between Succoth and Zarthan."

adults.question: What does the phrase "Solomon left all the vessels unweighed" indicate about the quantity of brass used?
adults.option_a: The brass was exceeding much; the weight could not be found out
adults.option_b: The priests deemed weighing unnecessary after the dedication
adults.option_c: The craftsmen lost count during production
adults.option_d: Only approximate totals were kept in the royal records
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 7:47 — "And Solomon left all the vessels unweighed, because they were exceeding many: neither was the weight of the brass found out."

kids.question: What holy object was brought into the temple when it was dedicated?
kids.option_a: The ark of the covenant of the LORD
kids.explanation: 1 Kings 8:1 — "Then Solomon assembled the elders of Israel...to bring up the ark of the covenant of the LORD out of the city of David."

kids.question: What filled the temple when the ark was placed inside?
kids.option_a: A cloud — the glory of the LORD
kids.explanation: 1 Kings 8:10-11 — "And it came to pass...that the cloud filled the house of the LORD...for the glory of the LORD had filled the house of the LORD."

kids.question: What were inside the ark of the covenant when it was brought to the temple?
kids.option_a: The two tables of stone which Moses put there at Horeb
kids.option_b: The rod of Aaron and a pot of manna
kids.option_c: The Ten Commandments and the book of the law
kids.explanation: 1 Kings 8:9 — "There was nothing in the ark save the two tables of stone, which Moses put there at Horeb."

teens.question: What was Solomon's declaration about God's dwelling place that showed humility?
teens.option_a: The heaven and heaven of heavens cannot contain thee; how much less this house that I have builded?
teens.option_b: Let the temple be thy dwelling forever
teens.option_c: God is too great for any building to contain him
teens.option_d: This house is too small for the LORD
teens.explanation: 1 Kings 8:27 — "But will God indeed dwell on the earth? behold, the heaven and heaven of heavens cannot contain thee; how much less this house that I have builded?"

teens.question: In his prayer, what did Solomon ask God to do when Israel was defeated because of sin but then repented?
teens.option_a: Hear their prayer, forgive, and bring them again to the land God gave their fathers
teens.option_b: Send a sign from heaven to confirm His forgiveness
teens.option_c: Raise up a prophet to guide the repentant people
teens.option_d: Remove the enemy nations from their borders
teens.explanation: 1 Kings 8:34 — "Then hear thou in heaven, and forgive the sin of thy people Israel, and bring them again unto the land which thou gavest unto their fathers."

teens.question: What offering did Solomon make at the temple dedication, and how large was it?
teens.option_a: Twenty-two thousand oxen and an hundred and twenty thousand sheep
teens.option_b: A thousand burnt offerings
teens.option_c: Seven days of daily sacrifices
teens.option_d: Three thousand oxen and sixty thousand sheep
teens.explanation: 1 Kings 8:63 — "And Solomon offered a sacrifice of peace offerings, which he offered unto the LORD, two and twenty thousand oxen, and an hundred and twenty thousand sheep."

teens.question: How long did the feast of dedication last?
teens.option_a: Fourteen days — seven days and then another seven days
teens.option_b: Seven days only
teens.option_c: One month of daily ceremonies
teens.option_d: Forty days to mirror Moses on Sinai
teens.explanation: 1 Kings 8:65 — "And at that time Solomon held a feast, and all Israel with him...seven days and seven days, even fourteen days."

teens.question: What did Solomon ask God to grant to foreigners who would come from far away and pray toward the temple?
teens.option_a: Do all that the stranger calleth to thee for, that all people may know God's name and fear Him
teens.option_b: Give them double the blessing of Israel
teens.option_c: Let them become part of Israel
teens.option_d: Hear their prayer only if they convert to Israel's faith
teens.explanation: 1 Kings 8:43 — "Hear thou in heaven thy dwelling place, and do according to all that the stranger calleth to thee for: that all people of the earth may know thy name."

adults.question: In what month and during what feast was the ark brought to the temple?
adults.option_a: The feast of the seventh month — the feast of tabernacles (Ethanim)
adults.option_b: The Passover feast in the first month
adults.option_c: The feast of weeks in the third month
adults.option_d: The new year feast at the start of Bul
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 8:2 — "And all the men of Israel assembled themselves unto king Solomon at the feast in the month Ethanim, which is the seventh month."

adults.question: What was the theological significance of the staves (poles) being visible at the holy place?
adults.option_a: They were a visible witness that the ark — God's presence — was permanently installed; they are there unto this day
adults.option_b: They indicated the ark was ready to be transported again
adults.option_c: They symbolised the two tablets of the covenant
adults.option_d: They were a priestly reminder of the wilderness journey
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 8:8 — "And the staves were so long, that the ends of the staves were seen out in the holy place before the oracle, and they were not seen without: and there they are unto this day."

adults.question: What did Solomon say God had fulfilled by the temple dedication that had been promised to David?
adults.option_a: God had fulfilled with His hand what He spoke with His mouth — the promise to David His servant
adults.option_b: The covenant land from Egypt to Euphrates was fully possessed
adults.option_c: Peace from all enemies had been perfectly achieved
adults.option_d: The number of Israel was as God had promised to Abraham
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 8:24 — "Who hast kept with thy servant David my father that thou promisedst him: thou spakest also with thy mouth, and hast fulfilled it with thine hand."

adults.question: What seven petitions does Solomon's prayer (vv. 31-53) address?
adults.option_a: Oath disputes, defeat in battle, drought, famine/plague, foreign prayer, war, and captivity
adults.option_b: Forgiveness, healing, rain, harvest, war, plague, and peace
adults.option_c: Individual sin, national sin, famine, drought, enemies, foreigners, and warfare
adults.option_d: Prayer in crisis, mercy for the repentant, justice for the wicked, healing for the sick, blessing for the righteous, peace for the land, and return from exile
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 8:31-53 — Solomon's dedicatory prayer contains seven petitions covering oath swearing, military defeat, drought, famine/pestilence, foreigner's prayer, war, and exile/captivity.

adults.question: What Exodus-rooted theology does Solomon invoke at the close of his prayer to ground his appeal?
adults.option_a: God separated Israel from all people of the earth to be His inheritance, as He said through Moses His servant
adults.option_b: God brought Israel out of Egypt with a mighty hand and a stretched-out arm
adults.option_c: God gave Israel the law at Sinai as a covenant of mercy
adults.option_d: God chose Israel because of Abraham's faith, not their own merit
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 8:53 — "For thou didst separate them from among all the people of the earth, to be thine inheritance, as thou spakest by the hand of Moses thy servant."

adults.question: What blessing did Solomon pronounce over Israel after his prayer?
adults.option_a: Blessed be the LORD that hath given rest unto his people Israel according to all that he promised; not one word of all his good promise hath failed
adults.option_b: May the LORD prosper you in all your ways
adults.option_c: The LORD your God will be with you as He was with our fathers
adults.option_d: Go and serve the LORD your God with all your heart and all your soul
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 8:56 — "Blessed be the LORD, that hath given rest unto his people Israel, according to all that he promised: there hath not failed one word of all his good promise."

adults.question: What final exhortation did Solomon give to Israel after the blessing?
adults.option_a: Let your heart be perfect with the LORD our God, to walk in his statutes and keep his commandments as at this day
adults.option_b: Return to your tents and rejoice in the LORD
adults.option_c: Fear the LORD and serve Him in truth and sincerity
adults.option_d: Remember what God did for your fathers in Egypt
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 8:61 — "Let your heart therefore be perfect with the LORD our God, to walk in his statutes, and to keep his commandments, as at this day."

adults.question: What happens to the priests when they cannot stand to minister because of the cloud, and what does this signify?
adults.option_a: The priests fell prostrate in worship and could not stand
adults.option_b: The priests were temporarily blinded by a heavenly light
adults.option_c: The weight of the cloud collapsed part of the structure
adults.option_d: The overwhelming presence of God's glory filling the house made priestly service impossible, showing God's actual indwelling
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 8:11 — "So that the priests could not stand to minister because of the cloud: for the glory of the LORD had filled the house of the LORD."

kids.question: Did God appear to Solomon again after the temple was built?
kids.option_a: Yes, a second time, as He had appeared at Gibeon
kids.option_b: No, God never appeared to Solomon again
kids.option_c: Yes, many more times throughout his reign
kids.option_d: No, only the prophets heard from God after that
kids.explanation: 1 Kings 9:2 — "That the LORD appeared to Solomon the second time, as he had appeared unto him at Gibeon."

kids.question: What did God promise to do to the temple if Israel turned away and worshipped other gods?
kids.option_a: Cast out Israel and the house shall be a proverb and a byword among all people
kids.option_c: Remove the ark of the covenant
kids.explanation: 1 Kings 9:7 — "Then will I cut off Israel out of the land which I have given them; and this house, which I have hallowed for my name, will I cast out of my sight; and Israel shall be a proverb and a byword among all people."

kids.question: What did Solomon give Hiram king of Tyre in return for all his help?
kids.option_a: Twenty cities in the land of Galilee
kids.option_b: Silver equal to the wood he sent
kids.option_d: Hiram's own son a place in the palace
kids.explanation: 1 Kings 9:11 — "Solomon gave Hiram twenty cities in the land of Galilee."

teens.question: What conditional warning did God give Solomon regarding the temple and the kingdom?
teens.option_a: If Israel forsook God's commandments and served other gods, He would cut off Israel and the temple would be a heap of ruins and a byword
teens.option_b: If Solomon built altars to foreign gods he alone would be punished
teens.option_c: The temple would stand only as long as the Levites served faithfully
teens.option_d: God would withhold rain until the people returned to the temple
teens.explanation: 1 Kings 9:6-7 — "But if ye shall at all turn from following me...then will I cut off Israel out of the land...and this house, which I have hallowed for my name, will I cast out of my sight."

teens.question: What was Hiram's reaction when he saw the twenty cities Solomon gave him?
teens.option_a: He was displeased and called them Cabul — a name meaning "good for nothing"
teens.option_b: He was greatly pleased and thanked Solomon
teens.option_c: He accepted them without comment
teens.option_d: He sent them back and asked for gold instead
teens.explanation: 1 Kings 9:12-13 — "And Hiram came out from Tyre to see the cities which Solomon had given him; and they pleased him not. And he called them the land of Cabul unto this day."

teens.question: What did Solomon do with the remnant of non-Israelite peoples still living in Canaan?
teens.option_a: He put them under tribute of bond service; he did not make the children of Israel servants
teens.option_b: He expelled them from the land completely
teens.option_c: He gave them equal status with the Israelites
teens.option_d: He put them all to the sword
teens.explanation: 1 Kings 9:20-22 — "And all the people that were left of the Amorites, Hittites...which were not of the children of Israel...upon those did Solomon levy a tribute of bondservice unto this day. But of the children of Israel did Solomon make no bondmen."

teens.question: What fleet did Solomon build at Ezion-geber and what was its significance?
teens.option_a: A navy of ships on the Red Sea shore in Edom, staffed with Hiram's shipmen alongside Solomon's men
teens.option_b: A river fleet on the Jordan for trade with the north
teens.option_c: A Mediterranean fleet based at Joppa
teens.option_d: A war fleet to guard the Euphrates border
teens.explanation: 1 Kings 9:26-27 — "And king Solomon made a navy of ships in Ezion-geber...on the shore of the Red sea...And Hiram sent in the navy his servants, shipmen that had knowledge of the sea, with the servants of Solomon."

teens.question: How much gold did the fleet bring back to Solomon from Ophir?
teens.option_a: Four hundred and twenty talents of gold
teens.option_b: A thousand talents of gold
teens.option_c: Three hundred talents of gold
teens.option_d: Six hundred and sixty-six talents
teens.explanation: 1 Kings 9:28 — "And they came to Ophir, and fetched from thence gold, four hundred and twenty talents, and brought it to king Solomon."

adults.question: What two-part divine affirmation did God give Solomon at the beginning of chapter 9?
adults.option_a: God had heard Solomon's prayer, had hallowed the temple as His name's dwelling forever, and His eyes and heart would be there perpetually
adults.option_b: God confirmed the Davidic covenant without conditions
adults.option_c: God promised Solomon military victory over all nations
adults.option_d: God extended Solomon's reign for an additional forty years
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 9:3 — "And the LORD said unto him, I have heard thy prayer and thy supplication...I have hallowed this house...to put my name there for ever; and mine eyes and mine heart shall be there perpetually."

adults.question: What threefold conditional promise did God make to Solomon personally in this second appearance?
adults.option_a: If Solomon walked before God as David had done, in integrity and uprightness, keeping His statutes, God would establish the throne of Israel forever
adults.option_b: God would make Solomon the wisest man of all time if he kept the law
adults.option_c: God promised long life, riches, and victory to Solomon personally
adults.option_d: God pledged ten additional tribes to Solomon's successors
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 9:4-5 — "And if thou wilt walk before me, as David thy father walked...then I will establish the throne of thy kingdom upon Israel for ever, as I promised to David."

adults.question: What stunning reversal does the name "Cabul" represent in the context of Solomon's alliance with Hiram?
adults.option_a: A diplomatic embarrassment — Solomon's payment in land failed to satisfy Hiram, undermining the friendship
adults.option_b: A legal boundary dispute that led to war
adults.option_c: A sign that Solomon had no more land to give
adults.option_d: A deliberate insult from Solomon to Hiram
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 9:13 — "And he said, What cities are these which thou hast given me, my brother? And he called them the land of Cabul unto this day."

adults.question: How many workers did Solomon assign to oversee the labour on his building projects, and how were they categorised?
adults.option_a: Three thousand three hundred chief officers who bore rule over the people — distinguished from the 550 who ruled over the chief work
adults.option_b: Twelve governors, one per tribe
adults.option_c: Seventy elders appointed as overseers
adults.option_d: Five hundred officials with the high priest as chief overseer
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 9:23 — "These were the chief of the officers that were over Solomon's work, five hundred and fifty, which bare rule over the people that wrought in the work."

adults.question: What was the theological purpose of the three annual occasions when Solomon offered burnt offerings at the temple?
adults.option_a: He offered on the three feasts — Passover, Pentecost (Weeks), and Tabernacles — the appointed sacred calendar of Israel
adults.option_b: He offered monthly to mark each new moon
adults.option_c: He offered weekly every Sabbath before the ark
adults.option_d: He offered only when returning from military campaigns
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 9:25 — "And three times in a year did Solomon offer burnt offerings and peace offerings upon the altar which he built unto the LORD...on the three feasts."

adults.question: What building projects beyond the temple and palace does chapter 9 record Solomon completing?
adults.option_a: The Millo, the wall of Jerusalem, Hazor, Megiddo, Gezer, Lower Beth-horon, Baalath, and Tadmor
adults.option_b: Only the palace and the temple complex were listed
adults.option_c: Storehouses and granaries in every tribe's territory
adults.option_d: Forts along the Egyptian border and the coast
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 9:15-18 — "And this is the reason of the levy...to build the house of the LORD, and his own house, and Millo, and the wall of Jerusalem, and Hazor, and Megiddo, and Gezer."

adults.question: Why was the city of Gezer included in Solomon's building programme?
adults.option_a: Pharaoh had taken it, burned it, killed its Canaanite inhabitants, and given it as a dowry to Solomon's Egyptian wife
adults.option_b: Solomon conquered it himself in a military campaign
adults.option_c: It was purchased from the Canaanites for silver
adults.option_d: It was given to Solomon by Hiram of Tyre as part of their treaty
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 9:16 — "For Pharaoh king of Egypt had gone up, and taken Gezer, and burnt it with fire, and slain the Canaanites that dwelt in the city, and given it for a present unto his daughter, Solomon's wife."

adults.question: What does Solomon's building of Tadmor in the wilderness and store cities in Hamath suggest about his reign's geographic reach?
adults.option_a: His empire and influence extended well beyond Canaan into northern Syria and the desert trade routes
adults.option_b: He was merely fortifying borders for defence
adults.option_c: These were religious centres for Israelite diaspora communities
adults.option_d: They were built to house the excess labour force from the levy
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 9:18-19 — "And Baalath, and Tadmor in the wilderness, in the land...And all the cities of store that Solomon had, and cities for his chariots, and cities for his horsemen."

kids.question: Who came from far away to test Solomon's wisdom with hard questions?
kids.option_a: The queen of Sheba
kids.explanation: 1 Kings 10:1 — "And when the queen of Sheba heard of the fame of Solomon concerning the name of the LORD, she came to prove him with hard questions."

kids.question: What did the queen of Sheba say when she saw all of Solomon's wisdom and riches?
kids.option_a: The half was not told me; thy wisdom and prosperity exceedeth the fame I heard
kids.option_b: Solomon is truly the greatest king in the world
kids.option_c: I have never seen anything like this in all my travels
kids.option_d: Blessed are thy men who stand before thee and hear thy wisdom
kids.explanation: 1 Kings 10:7 — "Howbeit I believed not the words, until I came, and mine eyes had seen it: and, behold, the half was not told me."

kids.question: What special throne did Solomon make?
kids.option_a: A great throne of ivory overlaid with the best gold
kids.option_b: A cedar throne with gold cushions
kids.option_c: A throne of pure gold with twelve pillars
kids.option_d: A bronze throne set with precious stones
kids.explanation: 1 Kings 10:18 — "Moreover the king made a great throne of ivory, and overlaid it with the best gold."

teens.question: What did the queen of Sheba bring Solomon when she visited?
teens.option_a: Spices, very much gold, and precious stones
teens.option_b: Silks and fine linen from the east
teens.option_c: Horses and chariots as tribute
teens.option_d: Ivory and rare birds
teens.explanation: 1 Kings 10:2 — "And she came to Jerusalem with a very great train, with camels that bare spices, and very much gold, and precious stones."

teens.question: What blessing did the queen of Sheba pronounce about the God of Israel?
teens.option_a: Blessed be the LORD thy God, which delighted in thee, to set thee on the throne of Israel
teens.option_b: Your God is the true God above all gods
teens.option_c: The LORD has given thee all wisdom and riches
teens.option_d: Blessed are the people whose God is the LORD
teens.explanation: 1 Kings 10:9 — "Blessed be the LORD thy God, which delighted in thee, to set thee on the throne of Israel: because the LORD loved Israel for ever, therefore made he thee king."

teens.question: How much gold did Solomon receive each year?
teens.option_a: Six hundred threescore and six talents of gold
teens.option_b: A thousand talents annually
teens.option_c: Five hundred talents from taxes and five hundred from trade
teens.option_d: Two thousand talents from the queen of Sheba alone
teens.explanation: 1 Kings 10:14 — "Now the weight of gold that came to Solomon in one year was six hundred threescore and six talents of gold."

teens.question: What was unique about the steps of Solomon's ivory throne?
teens.option_a: Six steps led to it with twelve lions, two on each side of the six steps
teens.option_b: It had seven steps representing the days of creation
teens.option_c: The steps were made of solid gold
teens.option_d: It was raised on a platform of carved cedar
teens.explanation: 1 Kings 10:19-20 — "The throne had six steps, and the top of the throne was round behind: and there were stays on either side...And twelve lions stood there on the one side and on the other."

teens.question: What was Solomon's trading fleet called and where did it sail?
teens.option_a: The navy of Tharshish bringing gold, silver, ivory, apes, and peacocks every three years
teens.option_b: The fleet of Ophir sailing monthly to Arabia
teens.option_c: The ships of Tarshish carrying cedar and fir to Egypt
teens.option_d: The eastern fleet trading silk and spices with India
teens.explanation: 1 Kings 10:22 — "For the king had at sea a navy of Tharshish with the navy of Hiram: once in three years came the navy of Tharshish, bringing gold, and silver, ivory, and apes, and peacocks."

adults.question: What does the text say the queen of Sheba's heart was like after Solomon answered all her questions?
adults.option_a: There was no more spirit in her
adults.option_b: She was filled with wonder and joy
adults.option_c: She was moved to tears
adults.option_d: She sat speechless for a long while
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 10:5 — "And when the queen of Sheba had seen all Solomon's wisdom, and the house that he had built...there was no more spirit in her."

adults.question: How many targets of beaten gold did Solomon make, and how much gold went into each?
adults.option_a: Two hundred targets of six hundred shekels of gold each
adults.option_b: A hundred targets of a thousand shekels each
adults.option_c: Three hundred shields of three hundred shekels each
adults.option_d: Five hundred targets of five hundred shekels each
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 10:16 — "And king Solomon made two hundred targets of beaten gold: six hundred shekels of gold went to one target."

adults.question: Where were the gold targets and shields stored that Solomon had made?
adults.option_a: In the house of the forest of Lebanon
adults.option_b: In the treasury of the temple
adults.option_c: In Solomon's palace armoury
adults.option_d: Under the throne room floor
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 10:17 — "And he made three hundred shields of beaten gold; three pound of gold went to one shield: and the king put them in the house of the forest of Lebanon."

adults.question: What material was used for all Solomon's drinking vessels, and what became of silver?
adults.option_a: All King Solomon's drinking vessels were of gold; silver was nothing accounted of in the days of Solomon
adults.option_b: Gold and silver vessels were used equally
adults.option_c: Only the temple vessels were gold; palace vessels were silver
adults.option_d: Gold was reserved for the temple while silver served the palace
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 10:21 — "And all king Solomon's drinking vessels were of gold...none were of silver: it was nothing accounted of in the days of Solomon."

adults.question: How many chariots and horsemen did Solomon accumulate, and where did he house them?
adults.option_a: One thousand four hundred chariots and twelve thousand horsemen in chariot cities and with the king in Jerusalem
adults.option_b: Two thousand chariots in forty stall cities
adults.option_c: A thousand chariots and ten thousand horsemen stationed at Megiddo only
adults.option_d: Three thousand chariots distributed among the twelve tribes
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 10:26 — "And Solomon gathered together chariots and horsemen: and he had a thousand and four hundred chariots, and twelve thousand horsemen...in the cities for chariots, and with the king at Jerusalem."

adults.question: What does the statement about Solomon's wealth echo regarding Deuteronomy's warnings to the king?
adults.option_a: It foreshadows the kingly violations Moses warned against — multiplying horses, gold, and wives — the very sins of chapter 11
adults.option_b: It proves Solomon fully obeyed Deuteronomy 17
adults.option_c: It shows God blessed Solomon beyond any previous king without conditions
adults.option_d: It demonstrates that wealth is always a sign of divine favour
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 10:14-29 — Solomon's multiplication of gold, chariots and horses directly echoes Deuteronomy 17:16-17's prohibitions against the king multiplying horses, gold, and wives.

adults.question: What trade route controlled by Solomon made him the middleman for horse and chariot commerce?
adults.option_a: He imported horses from Egypt and Kue, and kings of the Hittites and Syria bought through Solomon's merchants
adults.option_b: He monopolised the spice trade from Arabia
adults.option_c: He controlled the copper trade from the Arabah
adults.option_d: He dominated the grain trade from Egypt to Mesopotamia
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 10:28-29 — "And Solomon had horses brought out of Egypt, and linen yarn...and so for all the kings of the Hittites, and for the kings of Syria, did they bring them out by their means."
