adults.question: What superlative statement summarises Solomon's stature among the kings of his era?
adults.option_a: King Solomon exceeded all the kings of the earth for riches and for wisdom
adults.option_b: No king since David had such military power
adults.option_c: Israel was the mightiest empire in the world
adults.option_d: Solomon's temple made Jerusalem the centre of all worship
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 10:23 — "So king Solomon exceeded all the kings of the earth for riches and for wisdom."

kids.question: What did Solomon do that made God angry?
kids.option_a: He loved many foreign women and they turned his heart after other gods
kids.option_b: He stopped offering sacrifices at the temple
kids.option_c: He raised taxes on the poor
kids.option_d: He refused to listen to the prophets
kids.explanation: 1 Kings 11:4 — "For it came to pass, when Solomon was old, that his wives turned away his heart after other gods."

kids.question: What did God say He would do to Solomon's kingdom because of his sin?
kids.option_a: Rend the kingdom from Solomon and give it to his servant
kids.option_b: Destroy the temple he had built
kids.explanation: 1 Kings 11:11 — "Wherefore the LORD said unto Solomon...I will surely rend the kingdom from thee, and will give it to thy servant."

kids.question: Which tribe would remain with Solomon's son because of God's love for David and Jerusalem?
kids.option_a: One tribe
kids.explanation: 1 Kings 11:13 — "Howbeit I will not rend away all the kingdom; but will give one tribe to thy son for David my servant's sake."

teens.question: How many wives and concubines did Solomon have?
teens.option_a: Seven hundred wives, princesses, and three hundred concubines
teens.option_b: A hundred wives and fifty concubines
teens.option_c: Three hundred wives and seven hundred concubines
teens.option_d: Five hundred wives in all
teens.explanation: 1 Kings 11:3 — "And he had seven hundred wives, princesses, and three hundred concubines: and his wives turned away his heart."

teens.question: What false gods did Solomon follow because of his foreign wives?
teens.option_a: Ashtoreth of the Zidonians and Milcom of the Ammonites
teens.option_b: Baal and Asherah
teens.option_c: Chemosh and Molech
teens.option_d: Dagon and Rimmon
teens.explanation: 1 Kings 11:5 — "For Solomon went after Ashtoreth the goddess of the Zidonians, and after Milcom the abomination of the Ammonites."

teens.question: Who was the prophet that delivered God's message to Jeroboam about the torn kingdom?
teens.option_a: Ahijah the Shilonite
teens.option_b: Nathan the prophet
teens.option_c: Shemaiah of Judah
teens.option_d: Gad the seer
teens.explanation: 1 Kings 11:29 — "And it came to pass at that time when Jeroboam went out of Jerusalem, that the prophet Ahijah the Shilonite found him in the way."

teens.question: What did the prophet Ahijah do with his new garment to deliver his message to Jeroboam?
teens.option_a: He tore it into twelve pieces and told Jeroboam to take ten
teens.option_b: He wrapped Jeroboam in it as a sign of kingship
teens.option_c: He burned it before Jeroboam as a sign of God's judgment
teens.option_d: He folded it and placed it on Jeroboam's head
teens.explanation: 1 Kings 11:30-31 — "And Ahijah caught the new garment that was on him, and rent it in twelve pieces: And he said to Jeroboam, Take thee ten pieces."

teens.question: What three adversaries did God raise up against Solomon during his reign?
teens.option_a: Hadad the Edomite, Rezon son of Eliadah, and Jeroboam son of Nebat
teens.option_b: Hiram of Tyre, the queen of Sheba, and a Philistine king
teens.option_c: Pharaoh of Egypt, the king of Moab, and Abimelech
teens.option_d: A Philistine general, a Syrian commander, and an Ammonite prince
teens.explanation: 1 Kings 11:14,23,26 — "And the LORD stirred up an adversary unto Solomon, Hadad the Edomite...And God stirred him up another adversary, Rezon...And Jeroboam...lifted up his hand against the king."

adults.question: What was the precise commandment regarding foreign women that God had given Israel, and how is it cited in chapter 11?
adults.option_a: God had said Israel shall not go in to the nations, neither shall they come in unto Israel, for they will turn away your heart after their gods
adults.option_b: God had forbidden marriage outside the twelve tribes entirely
adults.option_c: God had permitted marriage to proselytes but not to worshippers of idols
adults.option_d: God had warned Solomon specifically in the dream at Gibeon against foreign marriage
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 11:2 — "Of the nations concerning which the LORD said unto the children of Israel, Ye shall not go in to them, neither shall they come in unto you: for surely they will turn away your heart after their gods."

adults.question: On what mountain did Solomon build high places for the abominations of Moab and Ammon?
adults.option_a: The hill that is before Jerusalem
adults.option_b: Mount Carmel
adults.option_c: The Mount of Olives
adults.option_d: Mount Zion
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 11:7 — "Then did Solomon build an high place for Chemosh, the abomination of Moab, in the hill that is before Jerusalem, and for Molech, the abomination of the children of Ammon."

adults.question: Why did God withhold the kingdom division from Solomon's own lifetime rather than tearing it in Solomon's day?
adults.option_a: For David his father's sake, God deferred the division to Solomon's son's time
adults.option_b: Because Solomon repented in his old age
adults.option_c: Because the temple still needed Solomon to maintain it
adults.option_d: Because no suitable replacement ruler had yet been found
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 11:12 — "Notwithstanding in thy days I will not do it for David thy father's sake: but I will rend it out of the hand of thy son."

adults.question: What was the background of Hadad the Edomite, and why was he a persistent thorn for Solomon?
adults.option_a: Hadad had escaped Joab's massacre in Edom as a child, fled to Egypt, married Pharaoh's sister-in-law, and returned to Edom to be adversary to Israel
adults.option_b: Hadad was a Philistine king who raided Israel's borders
adults.option_c: Hadad was a former Israelite officer who defected to Edom
adults.option_d: Hadad was a nephew of the king of Syria who claimed Israelite territory
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 11:17-22 — "That Hadad fled...into Egypt; Hadad being yet a little child...And Hadad found great favour in the sight of Pharaoh...And when Hadad heard in Egypt that David slept with his fathers...Hadad said to Pharaoh, Let me depart."

adults.question: What specific promise did Ahijah deliver to Jeroboam about the ten tribes?
adults.option_a: If Jeroboam walked in God's statutes and kept His commandments as David had done, God would build him a sure house as He built for David and give him Israel
adults.option_b: Jeroboam would rule unconditionally over ten tribes forever
adults.option_c: Ten tribes would follow Jeroboam only if he built them a new temple
adults.option_d: God would give Jeroboam ten tribes after Solomon's natural death
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 11:38 — "And it shall be, if thou wilt hearken unto all that I command thee, and wilt walk in my ways...then I will be with thee, and build thee a sure house, as I built for David, and will give Israel unto thee."

adults.question: What was Rezon's origin and how did he come to oppose Solomon?
adults.option_a: Rezon was a servant of Hadadezer king of Zobah who fled when David defeated his master, gathered men, and reigned in Damascus
adults.option_b: Rezon was an Edomite prince who unified the southern tribes against Israel
adults.option_c: Rezon was a renegade Israelite officer who allied with Egypt
adults.option_d: Rezon was the son of a Philistine king who had been dispossessed by David
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 11:23-24 — "And God stirred him up another adversary, Rezon the son of Eliadah, which fled from his lord Hadadezer king of Zobah...he was an adversary to Israel all the days of Solomon."

adults.question: What position did Jeroboam hold under Solomon before his rebellion, and why did Solomon seek to kill him?
adults.option_a: Solomon had made Jeroboam ruler over all the burden of the house of Joseph, and Solomon sought to kill him after Ahijah's prophecy
adults.option_b: Jeroboam was a military commander who refused orders
adults.option_c: Jeroboam was a priest who had spoken against the temple
adults.option_d: Jeroboam had led a tax revolt among the northern tribes
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 11:28,40 — "And the man Jeroboam was a mighty man of valour: and Solomon seeing the young man that he was industrious, he made him ruler over all the charge of the house of Joseph...Solomon sought therefore to kill Jeroboam."

adults.question: How long did Solomon reign in Jerusalem over all Israel, and where is more information about his acts recorded?
adults.option_a: Forty years, and the rest of his acts are in the book of the acts of Solomon
adults.option_b: Thirty years in Hebron and ten in Jerusalem
adults.option_c: Twenty years recorded in the book of the kings of Israel
adults.option_d: Fifty years, recorded in the chronicles of Nathan the prophet
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 11:42-41 — "And the time that Solomon reigned in Jerusalem over all Israel was forty years...And the rest of the acts of Solomon, and all that he did, and his wisdom, are they not written in the book of the acts of Solomon?"

kids.question: What did Rehoboam use to describe how he would punish the people compared to his father Solomon?
kids.option_a: My father chastised you with whips, but I will chastise you with scorpions
kids.option_b: My father was kind, but I will be kinder
kids.option_c: My father used swords, but I will use spears
kids.option_d: My father taxed you little, but I will tax you nothing
kids.explanation: 1 Kings 12:11 — "And now whereas my father did lade you with a heavy yoke, I will add to your yoke: my father hath chastised you with whips, but I will chastise you with scorpions."

kids.question: Who became king over the ten northern tribes when the kingdom split?
kids.option_a: Jeroboam
kids.explanation: 1 Kings 12:20 — "And it came to pass, when all Israel heard that Jeroboam was come again, that they sent and called him unto the congregation, and made him king over all Israel."

kids.question: What animals did Jeroboam make for the people to worship?
kids.option_a: Two golden calves
kids.explanation: 1 Kings 12:28 — "Whereupon the king took counsel, and made two calves of gold, and said unto them, It is too much for you to go up to Jerusalem: behold thy gods, O Israel, which brought thee up out of the land of Egypt."

teens.question: What advice did the old elders give Rehoboam that he rejected?
teens.option_a: To serve the people and speak good words to them so they would serve him forever
teens.option_b: To raise taxes immediately
teens.option_c: To build a stronger army
teens.option_d: To move the capital city
teens.explanation: 1 Kings 12:7 — "And they spake unto him, saying, If thou wilt be a servant unto this people this day, and wilt serve them, and answer them, and speak good words to them, then they will be thy servants for ever."

teens.question: What was the rallying cry of Israel when they rebelled against Rehoboam?
teens.option_a: "What portion have we in David? neither have we inheritance in the son of Jesse: to your tents, O Israel"
teens.option_b: "God save the king of Israel"
teens.option_c: "Down with the house of Solomon"
teens.option_d: "We will follow Jeroboam to war"
teens.explanation: 1 Kings 12:16 — "So when all Israel saw that the king hearkened not unto them, the people answered the king, saying, What portion have we in David? neither have we inheritance in the son of Jesse: to your tents, O Israel: now see to thine own house, David."

teens.question: Why did Jeroboam set up golden calves at Dan and Bethel?
teens.option_a: So the people would not go to Jerusalem to worship and return their hearts to Rehoboam
teens.option_b: Because Moses had commanded it
teens.option_c: Because God appeared to him in a vision
teens.option_d: Because the Levites requested new altars
teens.explanation: 1 Kings 12:27 — "If this people go up to do sacrifice in the house of the LORD at Jerusalem, then shall the heart of this people turn again unto their lord, even unto Rehoboam king of Judah."

teens.question: How long did Rehoboam wait before consulting advisors about attacking Israel to reunite the kingdom?
teens.option_a: Three days, as the people requested
teens.option_b: One week
teens.option_c: One year
teens.option_d: One month
teens.explanation: 1 Kings 12:5 — "And he said unto them, Depart yet for three days, then come again to me. And the people departed."

teens.question: What message from God stopped Rehoboam from going to war against Israel?
teens.option_a: That the division was God's doing and they should not fight against their brethren
teens.option_b: That Jeroboam was stronger and would win
teens.option_c: That the people of Israel had repented
teens.option_d: That an enemy nation was approaching
teens.explanation: 1 Kings 12:24 — "Thus saith the LORD, Ye shall not go up, nor fight against your brethren the children of Israel: return every man to his house; for this thing is from me."

adults.question: What was the cause of the kingdom's division according to the narrator?
adults.option_a: It was a turn of affairs brought about by the LORD to fulfil the word He had spoken by Ahijah to Jeroboam
adults.option_b: It was purely a political miscalculation by Rehoboam
adults.option_c: It was Jeroboam's military superiority
adults.option_d: It was the failure of Solomon's economic policies
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 12:15 — "Wherefore the king hearkened not unto the people; for the cause was from the LORD, that he might perform his saying, which the LORD spake by Ahijah the Shilonite unto Jeroboam the son of Nebat."

adults.question: Who was Adoram and what happened to him when Rehoboam sent him to Israel?
adults.option_a: He was over the tribute and was stoned to death by all Israel
adults.option_b: He was a prophet who was ignored
adults.option_c: He was a general who surrendered
adults.option_d: He was a priest who was imprisoned
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 12:18 — "Then king Rehoboam sent Adoram, who was over the tribute; and all Israel stoned him with stones, that he died. Therefore king Rehoboam made speed to get him up to his chariot, to flee to Jerusalem."

adults.question: Over which tribes did Rehoboam continue to reign after the division?
adults.option_a: Only the tribe of Judah
adults.option_b: Judah and Benjamin
adults.option_c: Judah, Benjamin, and Simeon
adults.option_d: All twelve tribes
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 12:20 — "There was none that followed the house of David, but the tribe of Judah only."

adults.question: What was the sin of Jeroboam's new religious system beyond the golden calves?
adults.option_a: He made priests from among all the people who were not of the sons of Levi
adults.option_b: He abolished the Passover feast
adults.option_c: He destroyed the ark of the covenant
adults.option_d: He built a new temple in Samaria
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 12:31 — "And he made an house of high places, and made priests of the lowest of the people, which were not of the sons of Levi."

adults.question: What feast did Jeroboam institute and when did he set it?
adults.option_a: A feast in the eighth month, on the fifteenth day, like the feast in Judah — ordained by himself
adults.option_b: A feast in the first month to celebrate his coronation
adults.option_c: A weekly feast every Sabbath at Bethel
adults.option_d: A yearly fast on the Day of Atonement
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 12:32-33 — "And Jeroboam ordained a feast in the eighth month, on the fifteenth day of the month, like unto the feast that is in Judah, and he offered upon the altar. So did he in Bethel, sacrificing unto the calves that he had made."

adults.question: What did Jeroboam tell the young men who grew up with him rather than the elders?
adults.option_a: He accepted their harsh advice to increase the yoke beyond his father's burdens
adults.option_b: He rejected their counsel for the elders' wisdom
adults.option_c: He asked them to negotiate with Jeroboam
adults.option_d: He sent them as ambassadors to Egypt
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 12:10-11 — "And the young men that were grown up with him spake unto him, saying... My little finger shall be thicker than my father's loins."

adults.question: What does the placement of one calf in Dan and one in Bethel suggest geographically?
adults.option_a: They marked the northern and southern boundaries of the northern kingdom, creating a national worship system
adults.option_b: They were placed near the two largest cities for convenience
adults.option_c: They replaced the two tablets of the law
adults.option_d: They corresponded to the two cherubim in Solomon's temple
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 12:29 — "And he set the one in Bethel, and the other put he in Dan." Dan was at the far north and Bethel at the southern edge of the kingdom — a calculated religious geography.

adults.question: How does Jeroboam's golden calf proclamation echo the Exodus narrative?
adults.option_a: He repeated the words of Aaron at Sinai: "behold thy gods, O Israel, which brought thee up out of the land of Egypt" — a deliberate inversion of the Exodus
adults.option_b: He quoted the Song of Moses from Deuteronomy
adults.option_c: He referenced the pillar of cloud and fire
adults.option_d: He recited the Ten Commandments as a cover
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 12:28 — "Behold thy gods, O Israel, which brought thee up out of the land of Egypt" (cf. Exodus 32:4). The verbal parallel condemns Jeroboam by linking his act to Israel's greatest apostasy.

kids.question: A man of God came to Bethel and cried against the altar. What happened to Jeroboam's hand when he stretched it out to seize him?
kids.option_a: It dried up and he could not pull it back to him
kids.option_b: It was cut off by a sword
kids.explanation: 1 Kings 13:4 — "And his hand, which he put forth against him, dried up, so that he could not pull it in again to him."

kids.question: What happened to the altar at Bethel as a sign?
kids.option_a: It split apart and the ashes poured out
kids.explanation: 1 Kings 13:5 — "The altar also was rent, and the ashes poured out from the altar, according to the sign which the man of God had given by the word of the LORD."

kids.question: What animal killed the man of God after he disobeyed God's command?
kids.option_a: A lion
kids.explanation: 1 Kings 13:24 — "And when he was gone, a lion met him by the way, and slew him: and his carcase was cast in the way, and the ass stood by it, the lion also stood by the carcase."

teens.question: What future king did the man of God prophesy would desecrate Jeroboam's altar at Bethel?
teens.option_a: Josiah
teens.option_b: Hezekiah
teens.option_c: Jehu
teens.option_d: Asa
teens.explanation: 1 Kings 13:2 — "And he cried against the altar in the word of the LORD, and said, O altar, altar, thus saith the LORD; Behold, a child shall be born unto the house of David, Josiah by name."

teens.question: What were God's strict instructions to the man of God from Judah?
teens.option_a: He must not eat bread or drink water in Bethel and must return by a different way
teens.option_b: He must preach at every city in Israel
teens.option_c: He must stay in Bethel for seven days
teens.option_d: He must not speak to King Jeroboam
teens.explanation: 1 Kings 13:9 — "For so was it charged me by the word of the LORD, saying, Eat no bread, nor drink water, nor turn again by the same way that thou camest."

teens.question: How did the old prophet of Bethel deceive the man of God into returning with him?
teens.option_a: He falsely claimed an angel had told him to bring the man back to eat and drink with him
teens.option_b: He offered him gold and silver
teens.option_c: He threatened to report him to the king
teens.option_d: He told him there was a shorter road home
teens.explanation: 1 Kings 13:18 — "He said unto him, I am a prophet also as thou art; and an angel spake unto me by the word of the LORD, saying, Bring him back with thee into thine house, that he may eat bread and drink water. But he lied unto him."

teens.question: What did the old prophet say when he saw the man of God's body after the lion killed him?
teens.option_a: He mourned and said the man of God was disobedient and God had delivered him to the lion
teens.option_b: He rejoiced because his deception had worked
teens.option_c: He buried him in his own tomb and mourned him as his brother
teens.option_d: He asked God to raise him from the dead
teens.explanation: 1 Kings 13:26 — "And when the prophet that brought him back from the way heard thereof, he said, It is the man of God, who was disobedient unto the word of the LORD: therefore the LORD hath delivered him unto the lion, which hath torn him, and slain him."

teens.question: What remarkable detail showed this was a supernatural event involving the lion?
teens.option_a: The lion did not eat the body and did not harm the donkey — it just stood by the carcase
teens.option_b: The lion spoke with a human voice
teens.option_c: The lion turned white after killing him
teens.option_d: The lion immediately disappeared
teens.explanation: 1 Kings 13:28 — "And he went and found his carcase cast in the way, and the ass and the lion standing by the carcase: the lion had not eaten the carcase, nor torn the ass."

adults.question: What specific prophecy about Josiah is given at the altar of Bethel, and how many years before its fulfillment was it spoken?
adults.option_a: That Josiah would burn human bones on it — spoken approximately 300 years before its fulfillment under Josiah (2 Kings 23)
adults.option_b: That Josiah would rebuild the altar in glory
adults.option_c: That Josiah would make Bethel the new capital
adults.option_d: That Josiah would find the Book of the Law there
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 13:2 — "Behold, a child shall be born unto the house of David, Josiah by name; and upon thee shall he offer the priests of the high places that burn incense upon thee, and men's bones shall be burnt upon thee." This was fulfilled around 300 years later in 2 Kings 23:15-16.

adults.question: How was Jeroboam's hand restored and what does this reveal about God's character in this episode?
adults.option_a: The man of God prayed and God healed it — showing God's mercy even toward apostate kings alongside His firm judgment
adults.option_b: It was healed by the old prophet's touch
adults.option_c: It healed naturally after three days
adults.option_d: It was never healed in the chapter
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 13:6 — "And the king answered and said unto the man of God, Intreat now the face of the LORD thy God, and pray for me, that my hand may be restored me again. And the man of God besought the LORD, and the king's hand was restored him again."

adults.question: What theological principle does the man of God's death illustrate about partial obedience?
adults.option_a: That disobeying God's word — even when deceived by a "prophet" — carries fatal consequences; believers are responsible to test all words against God's revealed command
adults.option_b: That prophets are always to be trusted
adults.option_c: That God punishes innocent mistakes without mercy
adults.option_d: That only kings are held accountable for disobedience
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 13:26 — "He is the man of God, who was disobedient unto the word of the LORD." The man had a direct word from God yet accepted a contradictory claim without testing it (cf. Galatians 1:8).

adults.question: Why did the old prophet request to be buried in the same tomb as the man of God?
adults.option_a: Because he believed the prophecy against Bethel's altar was true and wanted his bones to be spared alongside the man of God's bones
adults.option_b: Because he felt guilty for causing his death
adults.option_c: Because it was customary to bury prophets together
adults.option_d: Because his family tomb had no more room
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 13:31-32 — "Lay my bones beside his bones: For the saying which he cried by the word of the LORD against the altar in Bethel... shall surely come to pass." This was fulfilled in 2 Kings 23:17-18.

adults.question: What does the behavior of the lion — not eating the corpse and standing guard — communicate in its ancient Near Eastern context?
adults.option_a: It authenticated the divine judgment; the lion was an instrument of God's verdict, not a predator acting naturally, testifying to the supernatural nature of the punishment
adults.option_b: It showed the lion was tame and domesticated
adults.option_c: It indicated the man of God was poisonous
adults.option_d: It was a coincidence with no theological meaning
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 13:28 — "The lion had not eaten the carcase, nor torn the ass." In the ancient world, lions eating corpses was the ultimate desecration. The lion's restraint marked this death as divine judgment, not random violence.

adults.question: What was Jeroboam's response to all these signs — the withered hand, the restored hand, the split altar — regarding his sinful practices?
adults.option_a: He did not return from his evil way but made priests from all classes of people for the high places
adults.option_b: He repented and removed the golden calves
adults.option_c: He sought Ahijah the prophet for guidance
adults.option_d: He went to Jerusalem to worship at the temple
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 13:33 — "After this thing Jeroboam returned not from his evil way, but made again of the lowest of the people priests of the high places."

adults.question: What did the old prophet say to his sons before retrieving the body, and what does this reveal?
adults.option_a: He told them to saddle the donkey — his quick response and mourning suggest he knew the word against Bethel was genuine, making his deception all the more culpable
adults.option_b: He commanded them to warn the king
adults.option_c: He told them to inform Jerusalem
adults.option_d: He asked them to pray for the dead man's resurrection
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 13:27 — "And he spake to his sons, saying, Saddle me the ass. And they saddled him." His prompt mourning (v.30) reveals he accepted the man's prophetic authenticity — exposing his own deception as inexcusable.

adults.question: How does this chapter function as a literary hinge in 1 Kings regarding the fate of Jeroboam's dynasty?
adults.option_a: It names the ultimate executor of judgment (Josiah) before describing Jeroboam's continued apostasy, framing all subsequent northern kingdom history under the shadow of inevitable divine reckoning
adults.option_b: It simply records a curious incident with a lion
adults.option_c: It shows God was indifferent to Jeroboam's sins
adults.option_d: It suggests Bethel would eventually become a holy site
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 13:2, 33-34 — The Josiah prophecy and the editorial note that this "became sin unto the house of Jeroboam, even to cut it off, and to destroy it from off the face of the earth" bracket the chapter with inescapable doom.

kids.question: Why did Jeroboam send his wife to the prophet Ahijah?
kids.option_a: Because his son was sick and he wanted to know if the boy would recover
kids.option_b: Because he wanted to learn a new prophecy
kids.option_c: Because she wanted to become a prophetess
kids.option_d: Because Ahijah had asked to see her
kids.explanation: 1 Kings 14:1-3 — "At that time Abijah the son of Jeroboam fell sick. And Jeroboam said to his wife, Arise, I pray thee, and disguise thyself, that thou be not known to be the wife of Jeroboam; and get thee to Shiloh: behold, there is Ahijah the prophet."

kids.question: How did the blind prophet Ahijah know who was at the door before she told him?
kids.option_a: God had told him she was coming and what to say
kids.option_c: A servant had seen her coming
kids.explanation: 1 Kings 14:5 — "And the LORD said unto Ahijah, Behold, the wife of Jeroboam cometh to ask a thing of thee for her son; for he is sick: thus and thus shalt thou say unto her."

kids.question: What foreign king attacked Jerusalem and took away the treasures of the temple during Rehoboam's reign?
kids.option_a: Shishak king of Egypt
kids.explanation: 1 Kings 14:25-26 — "And it came to pass in the fifth year of king Rehoboam, that Shishak king of Egypt came up against Jerusalem: And he took away the treasures of the house of the LORD, and the treasures of the king's house."

teens.question: What did Ahijah prophesy would happen to Jeroboam's son Abijah?
teens.option_a: He would die when his mother's feet entered the city, and he alone of Jeroboam's house would come to the grave in peace
teens.option_b: He would recover and become a great king
teens.option_c: He would be carried to Jerusalem to be healed
teens.option_d: He would outlive his father and become king
teens.explanation: 1 Kings 14:12-13 — "Arise thou therefore, get thee to thine own house: and when thy feet enter into the city, the child shall die... And all Israel shall mourn for him, and bury him: for he only of Jeroboam shall come to the grave."

teens.question: What did Ahijah say God compared Jeroboam's sins to?
teens.option_a: All the evil of those who were before him — he had cast God behind his back and made other gods
teens.option_b: The sins of Pharaoh in Egypt
teens.option_c: The sins of the Canaanites before Israel
teens.option_d: The minor sins of David
teens.explanation: 1 Kings 14:9 — "But hast done evil above all that were before thee: for thou hast gone and made thee other gods, and molten images, to provoke me to anger, and hast cast me behind thy back."

teens.question: What does Ahijah say God will do to the house of Jeroboam?
teens.option_a: Cut it off and take it away as a man takes away dung, until it is all gone
teens.option_b: Restore it if Jeroboam repents
teens.option_c: Preserve it for David's sake
teens.option_d: Transfer the throne to another tribe
teens.explanation: 1 Kings 14:10 — "Therefore, behold, I will bring evil upon the house of Jeroboam, and will cut off from Jeroboam him that pisseth against the wall... as a man taketh away dung, till it be all gone."

teens.question: What sins of Judah under Rehoboam are mentioned in this chapter?
teens.option_a: High places, images, groves, and sodomites in the land — provoking God more than all their fathers
teens.option_b: Only idol worship at the temple
teens.option_c: Neglect of the Sabbath
teens.option_d: Refusal to pay tithes
teens.explanation: 1 Kings 14:23-24 — "For they also built them high places, and images, and groves, on every high hill, and under every green tree. And there were also sodomites in the land: and they did according to all the abominations of the nations."

teens.question: What replaced the golden shields Solomon had made after Shishak took them away?
teens.option_a: Rehoboam made bronze shields in their place
teens.option_b: He made new golden ones immediately
teens.option_c: He left the entrances unguarded
teens.option_d: He bought shields from Egypt
teens.explanation: 1 Kings 14:27 — "And king Rehoboam made in their stead brasen shields, and committed them into the hands of the chief of the guard."

adults.question: What was the theological irony of Jeroboam disguising his wife to consult Ahijah?
adults.option_a: He feared the prophet who had originally given him the kingdom, revealing that he knew his idolatry had forfeited God's favour — his deception showed guilt, not faith
adults.option_b: He simply did not want political embarrassment
adults.option_c: He was testing the prophet's abilities
adults.option_d: He respected the prophet too much to impose on him
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 14:2 — "Arise, I pray thee, and disguise thyself, that thou be not known to be the wife of Jeroboam." It was Ahijah who had torn the garment and promised Jeroboam the kingdom (1 Kings 11:29-31), making this consultation deeply ironic.

adults.question: What did Ahijah say God had originally offered Jeroboam that he had failed to receive?
adults.option_a: To build him a sure house as David's if he kept God's commandments — the same conditional promise given to David
adults.option_b: Unconditional rulership over all twelve tribes
adults.option_c: A special priestly dynasty
adults.option_d: Permanent protection from all enemies
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 14:7-8 — "I exalted thee from among the people, and made thee prince over my people Israel, And rent the kingdom away from the house of David, and gave it thee: and yet thou hast not been as my servant David."

adults.question: How does Ahijah describe what would happen to Israel because of Jeroboam's sins?
adults.option_a: God would smite Israel as a reed is shaken in water, root them up from the good land, and scatter them beyond the river
adults.option_b: God would send a single battle to discipline them
adults.option_c: God would temporarily remove their king
adults.option_d: God would send seven years of famine
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 14:15 — "For the LORD shall smite Israel, as a reed is shaken in the water, and he shall root up Israel out of this good land, which he gave to their fathers, and shall scatter them beyond the river."

adults.question: The phrase "beyond the river" in Ahijah's prophecy is a reference to what eventual historical event?
adults.option_a: The Assyrian exile — the deportation of northern Israel beyond the Euphrates, fulfilled in 722 BC under Shalmaneser V and Sargon II
adults.option_b: The Babylonian captivity of Judah
adults.option_c: A temporary military defeat by Syria
adults.option_d: The Exodus from Egypt in reverse
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 14:15 — "He shall root up Israel out of this good land... and shall scatter them beyond the river." This was fulfilled when Assyria deported the northern tribes (2 Kings 17:6), approximately 200 years after this prophecy.

adults.question: What does the narrative's description of Rehoboam's mother as "Naamah an Ammonitess" contribute theologically?
adults.option_a: It links Rehoboam's idolatrous reign back to Solomon's foreign wives and their foreign gods — the sin of the father bearing fruit in the son
adults.option_b: It is simply a genealogical notation
adults.option_c: It demonstrates Rehoboam's diplomatic heritage
adults.option_d: It shows God's grace to foreign nations
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 14:21 — "And his mother's name was Naamah an Ammonitess." Solomon's Ammonite wives had led him to Molech worship (1 Kings 11:1,7). Rehoboam's mother was one of those women, explaining the spiritual trajectory of his reign.

adults.question: What continual state existed between Rehoboam and Jeroboam throughout their reigns?
adults.option_a: Continual war — there was war between Rehoboam and Jeroboam all their days
adults.option_b: An uneasy peace maintained by treaty
adults.option_c: Regular diplomatic exchanges
adults.option_d: Occasional border skirmishes only
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 14:30 — "And there was war between Rehoboam and Jeroboam all their days."

adults.question: What does Ahijah's description of Israel's sins as groves (Asherah poles) on "every high hill and under every green tree" echo?
adults.option_a: The Deuteronomic warnings against precisely this form of Canaanite worship — showing Israel had fully adopted the abominations God had told them to destroy
adults.option_b: A neutral description of landscaping practices
adults.option_c: The fulfillment of the planting festivals
adults.option_d: A positive sign of agricultural prosperity
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 14:23 — "They also built them high places, and images, and groves, on every high hill, and under every green tree." Compare Deuteronomy 12:2: "Ye shall utterly destroy all the places, wherein the nations which ye shall possess served their gods, upon the high mountains."

adults.question: What does the replacement of Solomon's golden shields with bronze shields symbolise in the broader narrative of 1 Kings?
adults.option_a: The progressive material and spiritual decline of the Davidic kingdom — from gold to bronze mirrors the trajectory from Solomon's glory to Rehoboam's diminished reign
adults.option_b: Simply a practical economic decision
adults.option_c: A military improvement since bronze was stronger
adults.option_d: God's judgment specifically on luxury
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 14:26-27 — Solomon had "made two hundred targets of beaten gold" (1 Kings 10:16), which Shishak took. Rehoboam made brass replacements — a vivid symbol of the kingdom's decline from Solomonic splendour.

kids.question: What good king of Judah removed idols and tried to follow God like his ancestor David?
kids.option_a: Asa
kids.explanation: 1 Kings 15:11 — "And Asa did that which was right in the eyes of the LORD, as did David his father."

kids.question: What relative did King Asa remove from being queen because she had made an idol?
kids.option_a: His grandmother Maachah
kids.explanation: 1 Kings 15:13 — "And also Maachah his mother, even her he removed from being queen, because she had made an idol in a grove; and Asa destroyed her idol, and burnt it by the brook Kidron."

kids.question: Who became king of Israel after Jeroboam's son Nadab was killed?
kids.option_a: Baasha
kids.explanation: 1 Kings 15:28 — "Even in the third year of Asa king of Judah did Baasha slay him, and reigned in his stead."

teens.question: Why was Abijam's heart not perfect with God, yet God still gave him a lamp in Jerusalem?
teens.option_a: For David's sake — God had promised to preserve David's line in Jerusalem
teens.option_b: Because Abijam built a great temple
teens.option_c: Because the people loved him greatly
teens.option_d: Because he defeated all his enemies
teens.explanation: 1 Kings 15:4-5 — "Nevertheless for David's sake did the LORD his God give him a lamp in Jerusalem... Because David did that which was right in the eyes of the LORD, and turned not aside from any thing that he commanded him all the days of his life, save only in the matter of Uriah the Hittite."

teens.question: What items did Asa bring into the house of the LORD?
teens.option_a: The things his father had dedicated, and silver and gold and vessels
teens.option_b: New golden lampstands he had made himself
teens.option_c: The ark from Shiloh
teens.option_d: Spices and incense from Arabia
teens.explanation: 1 Kings 15:15 — "And he brought in the things which his father had dedicated, and the things which himself had dedicated, into the house of the LORD, silver, and gold, and vessels."

teens.question: What deal did Asa of Judah make with Ben-hadad king of Syria?
teens.option_a: He sent silver and gold to Ben-hadad asking him to break his league with Baasha and attack northern Israel instead
teens.option_b: He gave Ben-hadad his daughter in marriage
teens.option_c: He agreed to pay yearly tribute to Syria
teens.option_d: He offered Ben-hadad cities in the Negev
teens.explanation: 1 Kings 15:18-19 — "Then Asa took all the silver and the gold that were left in the treasures of the house of the LORD, and the treasures of the king's house, and delivered them into the hand of his servants: and king Asa sent them to Ben-hadad... saying, There is a league between me and thee."

teens.question: What did Baasha fulfil by wiping out all the house of Jeroboam?
teens.option_a: The prophecy of Ahijah the Shilonite concerning Jeroboam's house
teens.option_b: His own personal ambition without any prophetic significance
teens.option_c: A command given to him by Moses in the law
teens.option_d: A treaty obligation with Egypt
teens.explanation: 1 Kings 15:29 — "And it came to pass, when he reigned, that he smote all the house of Jeroboam; he left not to Jeroboam any that breathed, until he had destroyed him, according unto the saying of the LORD, which he spake by his servant Ahijah the Shilonite."

teens.question: What did Asa do with the materials Baasha had been using to build Ramah after Ben-hadad attacked Israel?
teens.option_a: He used the building stones and timber to build Geba of Benjamin and Mizpah
teens.option_b: He burned them all
teens.option_c: He sent them to Jerusalem for the temple
teens.option_d: He sold them to pay Ben-hadad
teens.explanation: 1 Kings 15:22 — "Then king Asa made a proclamation throughout all Judah; none was exempted: and they took away the stones of Ramah, and the timber thereof, wherewith Baasha had builded; and king Asa built with them Geba of Benjamin, and Mizpah."

adults.question: What does the repeated phrase "his heart was not perfect with the LORD his God" reveal about the Deuteronomistic evaluation of kings?
adults.option_a: It establishes wholehearted exclusive loyalty to Yahweh as the sole criterion of good kingship — a theological standard derived from the Shema (Deuteronomy 6:4-5)
adults.option_b: It refers only to ritual purity
adults.option_c: It measures kings by military success
adults.option_d: It evaluates economic policies
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 15:3 — "His heart was not perfect with the LORD his God, as the heart of David his father." The Deuteronomistic Historian uses this formula throughout 1-2 Kings as the primary lens for evaluating every monarch.

adults.question: What specific exception does the narrator make when praising David as the standard for kings?
adults.option_a: The matter of Uriah the Hittite — showing the narrator's historical honesty even while using David as the ideal benchmark
adults.option_b: David's taking of multiple wives
adults.option_c: David's failure to build the temple
adults.option_d: David's flight from Saul
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 15:5 — "David did that which was right in the eyes of the LORD, and turned not aside from any thing that he commanded him all the days of his life, save only in the matter of Uriah the Hittite." This parenthetical acknowledgement prevents an idealised reading of David.

adults.question: What does Asa's removal of the male cult prostitutes (sodomites) and idols suggest about the depth of religious corruption inherited from Rehoboam?
adults.option_a: It indicates that Canaanite fertility religion had been institutionalised in Judah since Rehoboam's reign and required deliberate royal action to dismantle
adults.option_b: It was a minor housekeeping matter
adults.option_c: It shows the people had chosen this worship freely
adults.option_d: It demonstrates Asa had authority only over religious matters
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 15:12 — "And he took away the sodomites out of the land, and removed all the idols that his fathers had made." Rehoboam's reign had introduced sodomites (1 Kings 14:24); Asa's reforms show how entrenched this had become in a single generation.

adults.question: What tension does Asa's alliance with Ben-hadad create theologically within his otherwise positive evaluation?
adults.option_a: He used the temple treasury to buy Syrian military help rather than trusting God — the Chronicler explicitly condemns this as faithlessness (2 Chronicles 16:7-9)
adults.option_b: It was a neutral diplomatic achievement
adults.option_c: It demonstrated wise foreign policy
adults.option_d: It fulfilled a Mosaic command about treaties
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 15:18 — "Then Asa took all the silver and the gold that were left in the treasures of the house of the LORD." The stripping of the temple treasury to fund a foreign alliance is theologically problematic, especially since the same treasury Shishak had not completely emptied was now voluntarily handed over.

adults.question: How does Nadab's assassination by Baasha fulfil Ahijah's prophecy and what pattern does it establish for the northern kingdom?
adults.option_a: It fulfils the word against Jeroboam's house and establishes a recurring pattern of violent dynastic change — unlike Judah where the Davidic covenant provided relative stability
adults.option_b: It was simply political instability with no theological meaning
adults.option_c: It shows God preferred Baasha to Jeroboam's line
adults.option_d: It established a tradition of peaceful succession
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 15:29-30 — "He smote all the house of Jeroboam... according unto the saying of the LORD." Every subsequent northern dynasty would end violently, in contrast to Judah's Davidic continuity, underscoring the importance of the Davidic covenant.

adults.question: What does the war between Asa and Baasha throughout their reigns reveal about the political geography of the divided kingdom?
adults.option_a: The border between Judah and Israel ran through Benjamin's territory near Ramah — a strategic flashpoint whose control determined the security of both capitals
adults.option_b: The two kingdoms had completely separate trade routes
adults.option_c: There was no economic interaction between the kingdoms
adults.option_d: The Philistines instigated all conflicts between them
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 15:16-17 — "And there was war between Asa and Baasha king of Israel all their days. And Baasha king of Israel went up against Judah, and built Ramah." Ramah was only five miles north of Jerusalem — Baasha's fortification there threatened to blockade the capital.

adults.question: How many years did Asa reign and what does this length suggest within the theological framework of 1 Kings?
adults.option_a: Forty-one years — among the longest reigns in Judah, which the narrative implicitly connects to his faithfulness, echoing the blessing-curse structure of Deuteronomy
adults.option_b: Twenty years, showing God's limited approval
adults.option_c: Twelve years, corresponding to the twelve tribes
adults.option_d: Eight years, a brief reign due to his sins
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 15:10 — "And forty and one years reigned he in Jerusalem." Asa's long reign stands in implicit contrast to wicked northern kings who ruled only briefly, though the text never makes this explicit — readers familiar with Deuteronomic theology would recognise the pattern.

adults.question: What does the phrase "nevertheless Asa's heart was perfect with the LORD all his days" suggest about the Deuteronomistic standard when compared to earlier kings?
adults.option_a: It means the high places were acceptable to God
adults.option_b: It means Asa committed no sins whatsoever
adults.option_c: It signals a wholehearted personal devotion even though Asa did not remove all the high places — the historian distinguishes between structural religious failures and sincere personal fidelity
adults.option_d: It applies only to Asa's last years
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 15:14 — "But the high places were not removed: nevertheless Asa's heart was perfect with the LORD all his days." This nuanced evaluation shows the historian can award positive verdicts despite incomplete reforms, provided personal loyalty to Yahweh is genuine.

kids.question: How many days did King Zimri reign over Israel before he died?
kids.option_a: Seven days
kids.explanation: 1 Kings 16:15 — "In the twenty and seventh year of Asa king of Judah did Zimri reign seven days in Tirzah."

kids.question: What wicked queen did King Ahab marry?
kids.option_a: Jezebel
kids.explanation: 1 Kings 16:31 — "And it came to pass, as if it had been a light thing for him to walk in the sins of Jeroboam the son of Nebat, that he took to wife Jezebel the daughter of Ethbaal king of the Zidonians."

kids.question: What false god did Ahab build a temple for in Samaria?
kids.option_a: Baal
kids.explanation: 1 Kings 16:32 — "And he reared up an altar for Baal in the house of Baal, which he had built in Samaria."

teens.question: What did the prophet Jehu son of Hanani prophesy against Baasha?
teens.option_a: That God would make his house like the house of Jeroboam because he walked in Jeroboam's ways and made Israel sin
teens.option_b: That Baasha would be victorious over Judah
teens.option_c: That his son would reign wisely
teens.option_d: That Israel would return to worshipping God
teens.explanation: 1 Kings 16:3-4 — "Behold, I will take away the posterity of Baasha, and the posterity of his house; and will make thy house like the house of Jeroboam the son of Nebat."

teens.question: How did Zimri seize the throne from King Elah?
teens.option_a: He killed Elah while he was drunk in the house of Arza his steward in Tirzah
teens.option_b: He led a military revolt at the border
teens.option_c: He challenged Elah to personal combat
teens.option_d: He bribed the palace guards
teens.explanation: 1 Kings 16:9-10 — "And his servant Zimri, captain of half his chariots, conspired against him, as he was in Tirzah, drinking himself drunk in the house of Arza steward of his house in Tirzah. And Zimri went in and smote him, and killed him."

teens.question: How did Zimri die when Omri's forces besieged Tirzah?
teens.option_a: He went into the king's palace and burned it over himself with fire
teens.option_b: He fled to Syria and was captured
teens.option_c: He was killed in battle at the city gate
teens.option_d: He surrendered and was executed
teens.explanation: 1 Kings 16:18 — "And it came to pass, when Zimri saw that the city was taken, that he went into the palace of the king's house, and burnt the king's house over him with fire, and died."

teens.question: What ancient curse was fulfilled when Hiel of Bethel rebuilt Jericho?
teens.option_a: Joshua's curse — he lost his firstborn when he laid the foundation and his youngest when he set up the gates
teens.option_b: A prophecy by Moses about rebuilding cities
teens.option_c: A curse from the time of the judges
teens.option_d: A warning given to Solomon about Jericho
teens.explanation: 1 Kings 16:34 — "In his days did Hiel the Bethelite build Jericho: he laid the foundation thereof in Abiram his firstborn, and set up the gates thereof in his youngest son Segub, according to the word of the LORD, which he spake by Joshua the son of Nun."

teens.question: How does the narrator summarise Ahab's wickedness compared to all the kings before him?
teens.option_a: He did more to provoke the LORD God of Israel to anger than all the kings of Israel that were before him
teens.option_b: He was slightly worse than Jeroboam
teens.option_c: He was equal in wickedness to Omri
teens.option_d: He was bad only because of Jezebel's influence
teens.explanation: 1 Kings 16:33 — "And Ahab made a grove; and Ahab did more to provoke the LORD God of Israel to anger than all the kings of Israel that were before him."

adults.question: What theological irony exists in God using Baasha to destroy Jeroboam's house and then pronouncing the same judgment on Baasha?
adults.option_a: Baasha was God's instrument of judgment against Jeroboam yet himself walked in Jeroboam's sins — the tool of judgment became subject to the same judgment, showing God's impartiality
adults.option_b: God made an error in choosing Baasha
adults.option_c: Baasha was simply unlucky in his successors
adults.option_d: God preferred the house of Omri over Baasha
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 16:7 — "And also by the hand of the prophet Jehu the son of Hanani came the word of the LORD against Baasha and against his house, even for all the evil that he did in the sight of the LORD... and because he killed him." Baasha committed the same sin he was used to punish.

adults.question: What does Omri's purchase of Samaria and its development as Israel's capital reveal about his political significance beyond the brief biblical account?
adults.option_a: Omri was a major regional power — Assyrian records called the northern kingdom "the house of Omri" for over a century after his dynasty ended, indicating his international importance
adults.option_b: Omri was insignificant and the capital choice was accidental
adults.option_c: Samaria had been a holy site since the judges
adults.option_d: The purchase was a sign of weakness
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 16:24 — "And he bought the hill Samaria of Shemer for two talents of silver, and built on the hill, and called the name of the city which he built, after the name of Shemer, owner of the hill, Samaria." The Assyrian "bit Humri" (house of Omri) designation demonstrates his lasting political legacy despite his brief biblical treatment.

adults.question: What does Ahab's marriage to Jezebel represent in terms of the religious trajectory of Israel?
adults.option_a: It introduced state-sponsored Phoenician Baal worship at the highest level, moving beyond Jeroboam's nationalist golden calf religion to wholesale foreign paganism
adults.option_b: A politically neutral diplomatic marriage
adults.option_c: A minor deviation from the norm of foreign wives
adults.option_d: An improvement since Jezebel brought cultural sophistication
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 16:31 — "He took to wife Jezebel the daughter of Ethbaal king of the Zidonians, and went and served Baal, and worshipped him." Ethbaal was a priest of Astarte who became king — Jezebel came from a priestly-royal family with total commitment to Phoenician religion.

adults.question: What pattern is established by the rapid succession of dynasties — Jeroboam, Baasha, Zimri, Omri — in the northern kingdom?
adults.option_a: Political instability through violent usurpation, contrasting sharply with Judah's Davidic covenant that provided dynastic continuity — the northern kingdom had no theological basis for stable succession
adults.option_b: Normal ancient Near Eastern political succession
adults.option_c: Evidence that God had abandoned Israel completely
adults.option_d: Proof that democracy was the preferred system
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 16:8-28 covers four kings in rapid succession, each overthrowing the previous. The Davidic covenant (2 Samuel 7) provided Judah with a theological framework for kingship that the north lacked entirely.

adults.question: How does the fulfillment of Joshua's Jericho curse (Joshua 6:26) in Hiel's building project function narratively at the end of 1 Kings 16?
adults.option_a: It demonstrates that ancient prophetic words retain their power centuries later, providing an ominous frame before Elijah's ministry — God's word stands regardless of time
adults.option_b: It is a historical curiosity with no narrative function
adults.option_c: It condemns Hiel's ambition but not Ahab's
adults.option_d: It shows the curse was weakened over centuries
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 16:34 — "According to the word of the LORD, which he spake by Joshua the son of Nun." Placed immediately before Elijah's appearance, this verse reactivates prophetic authority — if a centuries-old word proved true, Elijah's words would prove true too.

adults.question: What does Elah's drunkenness when killed suggest about the moral condition of the northern court under Baasha's dynasty?
adults.option_a: It portrays a leadership characterised by dissipation rather than vigilance — a negative counterpart to the warrior-king ideal of Deuteronomy and Samuel
adults.option_b: It was an isolated incident unrelated to his character
adults.option_c: Drunkenness was culturally acceptable for kings
adults.option_d: It shows Elah was betrayed by trusted servants
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 16:9 — "Drinking himself drunk in the house of Arza steward of his house in Tirzah." The detail of the king being drunk while his military commander plotted treason captures the dynasty's terminal weakness.

adults.question: How many verses does the narrator devote to Omri — one of Israel's most politically powerful kings — and what does this proportion reveal about the Deuteronomistic Historian's priorities?
adults.option_a: Only about eight verses — because Omri's significance is evaluated by obedience to God rather than political or military achievement, and he was extremely wicked
adults.option_b: Many chapters, showing his importance was fully recognised
adults.option_c: One verse, as he was considered irrelevant
adults.option_d: Half the chapter, balanced between politics and theology
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 16:23-28 — Omri gets roughly six verses despite founding the most internationally recognised Israelite dynasty. The historian's theological lens deliberately minimises rulers whose significance was purely political.

adults.question: What does the establishment of Baal worship in Samaria under Ahab and Jezebel set up for the ministry of Elijah in the following chapters?
adults.option_a: The theological confrontation between Yahweh and Baal — Elijah's entire ministry addresses whether Israel will serve the God of the covenant or the storm deity Baal, climaxing at Carmel
adults.option_b: A minor religious controversy quickly resolved
adults.option_c: Background information with no narrative consequence
adults.option_d: A political problem requiring diplomatic not prophetic solutions
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 16:32-33 — "He reared up an altar for Baal in the house of Baal... and Ahab did more to provoke the LORD God of Israel to anger than all the kings of Israel that were before him." This verse immediately precedes the introduction of Elijah (17:1), framing his ministry as Yahweh's direct response to Baal worship.

kids.question: What animals brought Elijah bread and meat while he hid by the brook Cherith?
kids.option_a: Ravens
kids.explanation: 1 Kings 17:6 — "And the ravens brought him bread and flesh in the morning, and bread and flesh in the evening; and he drank of the brook."

kids.question: What miracle happened with the widow's jar of meal and barrel of oil during the famine?
kids.option_a: They did not run out — the meal and oil lasted until rain came back to the land
kids.option_d: They multiplied into a storehouse of food
kids.explanation: 1 Kings 17:16 — "And the barrel of meal wasted not, neither did the cruse of oil fail, according to the word of the LORD, which he spake by Elijah."

kids.question: What did Elijah do to the widow's son who had stopped breathing?
kids.option_a: He stretched himself upon the child three times and prayed, and God brought him back to life
kids.option_b: He gave him medicine he had made
kids.option_c: He carried him to the city gate
kids.option_d: He called other prophets to help him
kids.explanation: 1 Kings 17:21 — "And he stretched himself upon the child three times, and cried unto the LORD, and said, O LORD my God, I pray thee, let this child's soul come into him again."

teens.question: What bold proclamation did Elijah make to Ahab at the very beginning of his ministry?
teens.option_a: That there would be neither dew nor rain in Israel except according to Elijah's word
teens.option_b: That Ahab must repent or be killed
teens.option_c: That the ten northern tribes would be given to Judah
teens.option_d: That Jezebel must leave Israel
teens.explanation: 1 Kings 17:1 — "As the LORD God of Israel liveth, before whom I stand, there shall not be dew nor rain these years, but according to my word."

teens.question: After the brook Cherith dried up, where did God send Elijah and why?
teens.option_a: To Zarephath in Sidon — God had commanded a widow there to sustain him
teens.option_b: To Jerusalem to hide in the temple
teens.option_c: To the wilderness of Sinai to pray
teens.option_d: To Egypt to find food
teens.explanation: 1 Kings 17:9 — "Arise, get thee to Zarephath, which belongeth to Zidon, and dwell there: behold, I have commanded a widow woman there to sustain thee."

teens.question: What was the widow of Zarephath preparing when Elijah arrived, and what was her plan?
teens.option_a: She was gathering sticks to make one last meal for herself and her son, expecting them to eat it and die
teens.option_b: She was preparing a feast for the village leaders
teens.option_c: She was baking bread to sell in the market
teens.option_d: She was cooking a sacrifice for Baal
teens.explanation: 1 Kings 17:12 — "As the LORD thy God liveth, I have not a cake, but an handful of meal in a barrel, and a little oil in a cruse: and, behold, I am gathering two sticks, that I may go in and dress it for me and my son, that we may eat it, and die."

teens.question: What faith challenge did Elijah give the widow before God sustained her household?
teens.option_a: He told her to make him a little cake first, then make for herself and her son — trusting God's promise that the meal and oil would not fail
teens.option_b: He asked her to pray with him for seven days
teens.option_c: He told her to share her food with all her neighbours first
teens.option_d: He required her to travel to the Jordan River
teens.explanation: 1 Kings 17:13 — "And Elijah said unto her, Fear not; go and do as thou hast said: but make me thereof a little cake first, and bring it unto me, and after make for thee and for thy son."

teens.question: What did the widow say to Elijah when her son died, and what does this reveal?
teens.option_a: She accused Elijah of coming to call her sin to remembrance and slay her son — revealing guilt and a belief in prophets as agents of divine judgment
teens.option_b: She calmly asked him to pray for the boy
teens.option_c: She said God had forsaken her because she was a Gentile
teens.option_d: She asked Elijah to leave immediately
teens.explanation: 1 Kings 17:18 — "And she said unto Elijah, What have I to do with thee, O thou man of God? art thou come unto me to call my sin to remembrance, and to slay my son?"

adults.question: What is the theological significance of Elijah being sent to Zarephath in Zidon — the homeland of Jezebel?
adults.option_a: It demonstrates that Yahweh's power and mercy extend beyond Israel even into the territory of Baal — God sustains a Gentile widow in Baal's own country while Israel suffers under the drought
adults.option_b: It was simply the closest place with food
adults.option_c: It showed Elijah was sympathetic to Jezebel's culture
adults.option_d: It had no theological significance beyond logistics
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 17:9 — "Arise, get thee to Zarephath, which belongeth to Zidon." Jesus later cited this in Luke 4:25-26: "unto none of them was Elias sent, save unto Sarepta, a city of Sidon, unto a woman that was a widow."

adults.question: How does the drought Elijah announces function as a direct polemic against Baal worship?
adults.option_a: The drought was only a political punishment
adults.option_b: Baal was the Canaanite storm and fertility deity responsible for rain and agricultural abundance — a drought under Yahweh's command was a direct demonstration that Baal had no power over weather
adults.option_c: It tested Ahab's agricultural management
adults.option_d: It had no connection to the Baal cult's claims
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 17:1 — "There shall not be dew nor rain these years, but according to my word." The drought was a frontal attack on Baal's core domain — if Baal could not produce rain in his own territory, his worship was exposed as worthless.

adults.question: What does the use of ravens to feed Elijah suggest about God's providential care?
adults.option_a: God commandeers unclean birds (Leviticus 11:15) — animals that would normally be ritually impure — to provide clean food, demonstrating that divine provision transcends ceremonial categories
adults.option_b: Ravens were considered holy birds in Israel
adults.option_c: The text is using "ravens" metaphorically for merchants
adults.option_d: It shows Elijah had trained the birds himself
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 17:6 — "And the ravens brought him bread and flesh in the morning, and bread and flesh in the evening." Ravens are listed as unclean in Leviticus 11:15. Their role as God's caterers highlights the extraordinary nature of divine provision.

adults.question: What does the phrase "the soul of the child came into him again, and he revived" establish theologically?
adults.option_a: It affirms Yahweh's authority over death itself — contrasting with Baal whose cult included death-resurrection mythology but who had no actual power over death
adults.option_b: A natural medical resuscitation
adults.option_c: A temporary suspension of death
adults.option_d: A sign relevant only to the widow personally
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 17:22 — "And the LORD heard the voice of Elijah; and the soul of the child came into him again, and he revived." This is one of three resurrections in the Elijah-Elisha cycle, demonstrating Yahweh's sovereignty over the realm of death that Baal mythology claimed.

adults.question: How does the widow's concluding confession compare to the typical Israelite response to Elijah's ministry?
adults.option_a: The Gentile widow confesses "Now by this I know that thou art a man of God, and that the word of the LORD in thy mouth is truth" — greater faith than most of Israel showed throughout Elijah's ministry
adults.option_b: It was a standard polite acknowledgement
adults.option_c: She remained skeptical even after the resurrection
adults.option_d: Her confession was weaker than Israel's response at Carmel
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 17:24 — "And the woman said to Elijah, Now by this I know that thou art a man of God, and that the word of the LORD in thy mouth is truth." The irony of a Gentile widow confessing what apostate Israel denied is a powerful narrative rebuke.

adults.question: What does the drying of the brook Cherith after Elijah had been sustained there teach about divine provision?
adults.option_a: The brook dried up due to normal weather patterns only
adults.option_b: God's means of provision are temporary and varied — He moves His servants to new places of dependence rather than allowing them to settle into relying on a fixed mechanism of supply
adults.option_c: It was a punishment for some sin of Elijah
adults.option_d: It showed the drought was not fully under God's control
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 17:7 — "And it came to pass after a while, that the brook dried up, because there had been no rain in the land." The very drought God sent to judge Israel also affected Elijah's hiding place, driving him to the next stage of his mission.

adults.question: What does Elijah's three-time stretching upon the child reveal about prayer and persistence in prophetic ministry?
adults.option_a: It models earnest, repeated intercession — Elijah did not receive immediate results but persisted in prayer, foreshadowing his seven-time prayer at Carmel (18:43)
adults.option_b: It was a standard medical procedure
adults.option_c: It showed Elijah doubted God's ability to heal
adults.option_d: It was a ritual inherited from Canaanite priests
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 17:21 — "And he stretched himself upon the child three times, and cried unto the LORD." Compare 1 Kings 18:43-44 where Elijah's servant goes seven times before the rain cloud appears — persistence in prayer is a recurring Elijah motif.

adults.question: How does 1 Kings 17 function as a preparation narrative for the confrontation in 1 Kings 18?
adults.option_a: It establishes Elijah's credentials through miracles that demonstrate Yahweh controls what Baal supposedly controlled — rain (drought), food supply (meal and oil), and life/death (resurrection) — making Carmel a climax, not a beginning
adults.option_b: It is merely biographical background
adults.option_c: It shows Elijah was unprepared for the Carmel confrontation
adults.option_d: It establishes only that Elijah was physically sustained
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 17 prepares for 18 in three ways: the drought challenges Baal's weather domain (17:1), the inexhaustible meal and oil mocks Baal's fertility claims (17:14-16), and the resurrection demonstrates Yahweh's power over death (17:21-22). Each miracle previews Yahweh's total superiority over Baal.

kids.question: How many prophets of Baal did Elijah challenge on Mount Carmel?
kids.option_a: Four hundred and fifty
kids.explanation: 1 Kings 18:22 — "Then said Elijah unto the people, I, even I only, remain a prophet of the LORD; but Baal's prophets are four hundred and fifty men."

kids.question: What happened when Elijah prayed and fire fell from heaven on Mount Carmel?
kids.option_a: The fire consumed the burnt sacrifice, the wood, the stones, the dust, and licked up the water in the trench
kids.option_b: The fire only burned the wood
kids.option_c: The fire lit a small flame on the altar
kids.explanation: 1 Kings 18:38 — "Then the fire of the LORD fell, and consumed the burnt sacrifice, and the wood, and the stones, and the dust, and licked up the water that was in the trench."

kids.question: What did Elijah see in the sky that meant rain was coming after the long drought?
kids.option_a: A little cloud like a man's hand rising out of the sea
kids.option_b: A rainbow across the whole sky
kids.option_c: Dark clouds covering all the mountains
kids.explanation: 1 Kings 18:44 — "And it came to pass at the seventh time, that he said, Behold, there ariseth a little cloud out of the sea, like a man's hand."

teens.question: What famous question did Elijah ask the people of Israel on Mount Carmel before the contest?
teens.option_a: "How long halt ye between two opinions? if the LORD be God, follow him: but if Baal, then follow him"
teens.option_b: "How long will you disobey the law of Moses?"
teens.option_c: "How many of you will fight for the LORD today?"
teens.option_d: "Why have you abandoned the temple in Jerusalem?"
teens.explanation: 1 Kings 18:21 — "And Elijah came unto all the people, and said, How long halt ye between two opinions? if the LORD be God, follow him: but if Baal, then follow him. And the people answered him not a word."

teens.question: What did the prophets of Baal do to try to get their god to answer?
teens.option_a: They leaped upon the altar, cried aloud, and cut themselves with knives and lancets until blood gushed out
teens.option_b: They sang psalms and burned incense
teens.option_c: They fasted for three days and nights
teens.option_d: They built a larger altar and offered more sacrifices
teens.explanation: 1 Kings 18:26,28 — "And they leaped upon the altar which was made... and they cried aloud, and cut themselves after their manner with knives and lancets, till the blood gushed out upon them."

teens.question: What did Elijah mockingly suggest might be keeping Baal from answering?
teens.option_a: That Baal was talking, pursuing, on a journey, or asleep and needed to be awakened
teens.option_b: That the sacrifice was too small
teens.option_c: That the wood was too wet
teens.option_d: That there were too few prophets present
teens.explanation: 1 Kings 18:27 — "And Elijah mocked them, and said, Cry aloud: for he is a god; either he is talking, or he is pursuing, or he is in a journey, or peradventure he sleepeth, and must be awaked."

teens.question: How did Elijah make the test harder for himself before calling on God?
teens.option_a: He had four barrels of water poured over the burnt sacrifice three times, filling the trench around the altar
teens.option_b: He used older, drier wood
teens.option_c: He offered a larger animal
teens.option_d: He prayed while standing far away from the altar
teens.explanation: 1 Kings 18:33-34 — "And he put the wood in order, and cut the bullock in pieces, and laid him on the wood, and said, Fill four barrels with water, and pour it on the burnt sacrifice... And he said, Do it the second time... And he said, Do it the third time."

teens.question: What did the people say when the fire fell and they saw the LORD had answered?
teens.option_a: "The LORD, he is the God; the LORD, he is the God"
teens.option_b: "Elijah is the greatest prophet"
teens.option_c: "We will serve Baal no more"
teens.option_d: "Send us rain, O LORD"
teens.explanation: 1 Kings 18:39 — "And when all the people saw it, they fell on their faces: and they said, The LORD, he is the God; the LORD, he is the God."

adults.question: Who was Obadiah and what had he done in secret during Jezebel's persecution of the prophets?
adults.option_a: He was over Ahab's household and feared the LORD greatly — he had hidden a hundred prophets in caves in groups of fifty and fed them with bread and water
adults.option_b: He was a priest who fled to Judah
adults.option_c: He was a prophet who preached openly against Jezebel
adults.option_d: He was a military captain who refused to hunt prophets
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 18:3-4 — "And Ahab called Obadiah, which was the governor of his house. (Now Obadiah feared the LORD greatly: For it was so, when Jezebel cut off the prophets of the LORD, that Obadiah took an hundred prophets, and hid them by fifty in a cave, and fed them with bread and water.)"

adults.question: What does Elijah's rebuilding of the twelve-stone altar symbolise?
adults.option_a: The restoration of the unity of all twelve tribes under Yahweh — a prophetic act declaring that the divided kingdom was one people before God
adults.option_b: The replacement of Jeroboam's golden calf altars
adults.option_c: A return to the Mosaic law of building stone altars
adults.option_d: A memorial to the twelve judges of Israel
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 18:31 — "And Elijah took twelve stones, according to the number of the tribes of the sons of Jacob... And with the stones he built an altar in the name of the LORD." On Carmel, in the northern kingdom, Elijah symbolically reasserted the unity of all Israel before God.

adults.question: What is the theological content of Elijah's prayer before the fire fell, and how does it differ from the Baal prophets' approach?
adults.option_a: He asked God to act so Israel would know He was God and that Elijah had done all things at God's word — it was a prayer for divine vindication and Israel's return to God, not a magical formula
adults.option_b: He repeated a formula seven times
adults.option_c: He prayed for personal victory over his enemies
adults.option_d: He asked God to punish Ahab and Jezebel
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 18:36-37 — "LORD God of Abraham, Isaac, and of Israel, let it be known this day that thou art God in Israel, and that I am thy servant, and that I have done all these things at thy word. Hear me, O LORD, hear me, that this people may know that thou art the LORD God, and that thou hast turned their heart back again."

adults.question: What does the phrase "the hand of the LORD was on Elijah" (v.46) mean and what does he do immediately after?
adults.option_a: He received supernatural strength and outran Ahab's chariot to Jezreel — a miraculous feat demonstrating that God's empowerment extends even to physical endurance
adults.option_b: He fell into a trance and received a vision
adults.option_c: He was carried in a chariot of fire
adults.option_d: He rested for three days after the contest
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 18:46 — "And the hand of the LORD was on Elijah; and he girded up his loins, and ran before Ahab to the entrance of Jezreel." Jezreel was roughly seventeen miles from Carmel — Elijah outran Ahab's chariot to this city.

adults.question: How does Elijah's persistent seven-times prayer for rain connect thematically to his earlier threefold stretching over the widow's son?
adults.option_a: Both episodes show that answered prayer often requires persisting through apparent silence — Elijah models the perseverance in intercession that Jesus later taught (Luke 18:1-8)
adults.option_b: Both show that prayer must use specific numbers
adults.option_c: Both occur in the same location near Zarephath
adults.option_d: Both involve miracles performed for Gentiles
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 18:43-44 — "And said to his servant, Go up now, look toward the sea. And he went up, and looked, and said, There is nothing. And he said, Go again seven times. And it came to pass at the seventh time, that he said, Behold, there ariseth a little cloud out of the sea, like a man's hand."

adults.question: What does the contest's resolution — fire consuming even the stones, dust, and water — reveal about the nature of Yahweh versus Baal?
adults.option_a: It demonstrates Yahweh's absolute sovereignty over all elements of nature including those Baal supposedly controlled — fire, water, earth — leaving no domain for Baal to claim
adults.option_b: It was simply a dramatic miracle for entertainment
adults.option_c: It showed Elijah had special fire-making abilities
adults.option_d: It meant Baal was a lesser but real deity
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 18:38 — "Then the fire of the LORD fell, and consumed the burnt sacrifice, and the wood, and the stones, and the dust, and licked up the water that was in the trench." The consuming of water (Baal's supposed element) and stone (the altar material) was a comprehensive territorial declaration.

adults.question: What happened to the prophets of Baal after the contest and who commanded it?
adults.option_a: Elijah commanded the people to seize them and he killed all four hundred and fifty at the brook Kishon
adults.option_b: They fled and were never found
adults.option_c: They were imprisoned by Ahab
adults.option_d: They were allowed to return to Samaria
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 18:40 — "And Elijah said unto them, Take the prophets of Baal; let not one of them escape. And they took them: and Elijah brought them down to the brook Kishon, and slew them there."

adults.question: How does Ahab's characterisation throughout chapter 18 — calling Elijah "he that troubleth Israel," obeying Obadiah's message, watching the contest, going to eat and drink — portray his leadership?
adults.option_a: As a passive, reactive, self-indulgent king who never takes theological initiative — his entire response to the greatest spiritual crisis in Israel's history is to consume food while Elijah intercedes
adults.option_b: As a repentant king who was misled by Jezebel
adults.option_c: As a secretly faithful king constrained by political pressures
adults.option_d: As a courageous military leader who respected Elijah
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 18:17-19, 41-42 — Ahab calls Elijah a troubler, passively attends the contest, and "went up to eat and to drink" while Elijah "cast himself down upon the earth, and put his face between his knees" praying for rain — a sharp contrast in spiritual seriousness.

kids.question: Why did Elijah run away into the wilderness after the great victory at Mount Carmel?
kids.option_a: Because Jezebel threatened to kill him within a day
kids.option_b: Because he was tired and needed rest
kids.option_c: Because God told him to leave Israel
kids.option_d: Because Ahab sent soldiers to arrest him
kids.explanation: 1 Kings 19:2-3 — "Then Jezebel sent a messenger unto Elijah, saying, So let the gods do to me, and more also, if I make not thy life as the life of one of them by to morrow about this time. And when he saw that, he arose, and went for his life."

kids.question: How did God feed Elijah when he was exhausted under the juniper tree in the wilderness?
kids.option_a: An angel touched him twice and gave him a cake baked on coals and a cruse of water
kids.option_c: A widow found him and fed him
kids.explanation: 1 Kings 19:5-6 — "And as he lay and slept under a juniper tree, behold, then an angel touched him, and said unto him, Arise and eat. And he looked, and, behold, there was a cake baken on coals, and a cruse of water at his head."

kids.question: What was the "still small voice" — how did God appear to Elijah at Horeb?
kids.option_a: After a great wind, an earthquake, and fire, God was not in any of those but spoke in a still small voice
kids.explanation: 1 Kings 19:12 — "And after the earthquake a fire; but the LORD was not in the fire: and after the fire a still small voice."

teens.question: What did Elijah ask God for when he sat under the juniper tree, and what does this reveal about his state of mind?
